title: Ready from Day 1: Population-Aware Coordination for Large-Scale Constrained Multi-Agent Systems
summary: In large-scale multi-agent systems with shared resource constraints, an upstream planner must iteratively evaluate candidate resource plans -- assessing feasibility, aggregate response, and marginal cost -- before committing to one. Lagrangian relaxation separates local decisions through a broadcast cost signal, but the planner still needs the cost-to-utilization response map to explore plan space, and this map depends on population composition that changes across planning cycles. We propose \emph{population-aware coordination interfaces}: learned primal and dual maps, conditioned on compact population summaries, that the planner queries inside its iterative loop. The primal map predicts aggregate utilization under a proposed cost trajectory; the dual map predicts the cost trajectory for a target plan. By encoding response-relevant population structure, these maps remain reliable across evolving populations without per-cycle retraining, and support coordination of large populations from compact subsamples. We additionally cast Sim2Real transfer as a backtestable procedure, enabling evaluation before deployment. In a supply-chain capacity-control case study, population-aware interfaces reduce forecast error by 16--19\% and capacity violations by 20--51\% relative to population-unaware baselines under composition shift; 20K-agent cohorts support accurate coordination of 500K-agent populations; and simulator-trained primal maps achieve 11.1\% MAPE on real observations versus 13--24\% for baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes population-aware coordination interfaces - learned primal and dual maps conditioned on population summaries - to enable efficient large-scale multi-agent coordination across changing populations without retraining.
AI.motivation: In large-scale multi-agent systems with shared resource constraints, planners need to evaluate resource plans by assessing feasibility, aggregate response, and marginal cost. Traditional Lagrangian relaxation requires cost-to-utilization response maps, but these maps depend on population composition that changes across planning cycles, requiring frequent retraining.
AI.method: Proposes population-aware coordination interfaces: learned primal and dual maps conditioned on compact population summaries. The primal map predicts aggregate utilization under proposed cost trajectories, while the dual map predicts cost trajectories for target plans. These maps encode response-relevant population structure to remain reliable across evolving populations without per-cycle retraining.
AI.result: In a supply-chain capacity-control case study: population-aware interfaces reduce forecast error by 16-19% and capacity violations by 20-51% relative to population-unaware baselines under composition shift; 20K-agent cohorts support accurate coordination of 500K-agent populations; simulator-trained primal maps achieve 11.1% MAPE on real observations versus 13-24% for baselines.
AI.conclusion: Population-aware coordination interfaces provide an effective solution for large-scale multi-agent coordination that remains reliable across changing population compositions without requiring frequent retraining, enabling efficient planning and transfer from simulation to real-world deployment.

title: Privacy Preserving Multi Agent Path Finding
comment: 16 pages, 5 figures, to be published in AAMAS 2026 as an extended abstract
summary: In the multi-agent path finding (MAPF) problem, a group of agents search in a graph for a path for each agent where no two paths collide. While in all applications of MAPF the agents must not collide with each other, in some of them the agents may not wish to share their paths due to privacy constraints. In this work, we formulate two types of privacy constraints for MAPF and propose algorithms that preserve them. The first type of privacy we consider is planning-level privacy, which means that during planning, the agents cannot identify exactly the planned location of the other agents. We propose a general framework for obtaining planning-level privacy, which works by adding mock agents to the planning process. The second type of privacy we consider is execution-level privacy, which is relevant when agents have limited sensing capabilities. Execution-level privacy is preserved if none of the agents is allowed to sense the location of the other agents during execution. We show how to adapt two popular MAPF algorithms, namely PIBT and LaCAM, such that they preserve execution-level privacy. Lastly, we propose a post-processing technique that allows the agents to reduce the sum of costs of the returned solution without losing any privacy. We also implemented our algorithms and evaluated them empirically, showing that the proposed post-processing technique indeed improved cost significantly.

AI.tldr: A framework for privacy-preserving multi-agent path finding (MAPF) that addresses both planning-level and execution-level privacy using mock agents, adapted algorithms, and post-processing optimization.
AI.motivation: While MAPF traditionally focuses on collision avoidance, many real-world applications require agents to maintain privacy by not sharing their exact paths with other agents. There is a need to address privacy constraints alongside collision avoidance in multi-agent systems.
AI.method: Two types of privacy constraints: 1) Planning-level privacy - prevents agents from identifying others' planned locations using mock agents during planning; 2) Execution-level privacy - prevents sensing of other agents' locations during execution by adapting existing MAPF algorithms (PIBT and LaCAM). Also includes post-processing optimization to reduce solution costs without compromising privacy.
AI.result: Proposed privacy-preserving frameworks for both planning and execution levels, with algorithm adaptations for existing MAPF methods. Post-processing technique significantly improved solution costs while maintaining privacy constraints, validated through empirical evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully addresses privacy concerns in MAPF through two novel privacy constraints, provides practical algorithms for both levels of privacy, and demonstrates that privacy-preserving solutions can be optimized without compromising privacy guarantees.

title: IFPV: An Integrated Multi-Agent Framework for Generative Operational Planning and High-Fidelity Plan Verification
summary: Operational plan generation and verification are critical for modern complex and rapidly changing battlefield environments, yet traditional generation and verification methods still respectively face the challenges of generation infeasibility and verification insufficiency. To alleviate these limitations, we propose an Integrated Multi-Agent Framework for Generative Operational Planning and High-Fidelity Plan Verification (IFPV). IFPV consists of two tightly coupled modules: Multi-Perspective Hierarchical Agents (MPHA) for generative operational planning and an Adversarial Cognitive Simulation Engine (ACSE) for high-fidelity adversarial plan verification. MPHA decomposes commander intent into executable multi-platform tactical action sequences through the collaboration of Pathfinder, Analyst, and Planner agents. ACSE introduces an opponent equipped with a customized world model, which predicts the future evolution of mission-critical platforms and conducts dynamic counteractions against candidate plans. Simulation experiments in the Asymmetric Combat Tactic Simulator (ACTS) show that IFPV improves mission success by 19.4% and reduces operational cost by 41.7% compared with a single-step large language model (LLM) planning baseline. Compared with a traditional rule-based validator, ACSE increases the average suppression rate by 31.8%, indicating that the proposed verification environment is stricter and more discriminative in revealing the latent vulnerabilities of candidate plans. The code for IFPV can be found at https://github.com/zhigao3ks/IFPV.

AI.tldr: IFPV is an integrated framework combining multi-agent operational planning with adversarial simulation verification for military battlefield environments, improving mission success by 19.4% and reducing costs by 41.7% compared to LLM baselines.
AI.motivation: Traditional operational plan generation faces feasibility issues while verification methods lack comprehensiveness, creating vulnerabilities in complex, rapidly changing battlefield environments that need more integrated and rigorous approaches.
AI.method: The IFPV framework has two coupled modules: 1) Multi-Perspective Hierarchical Agents (MPHA) with Pathfinder, Analyst, and Planner agents to transform commander intent into executable tactical actions; 2) Adversarial Cognitive Simulation Engine (ACSE) with opponent agents using customized world models to predict platform evolution and perform dynamic counteractions for plan verification.
AI.result: In the Asymmetric Combat Tactic Simulator (ACTS), IFPV improved mission success by 19.4% and reduced operational cost by 41.7% compared to single-step LLM planning. ACSE increased average suppression rate by 31.8% over traditional rule-based validators, showing stricter vulnerability detection.
AI.conclusion: IFPV provides a more integrated and rigorous approach to operational planning and verification, with the adversarial simulation component offering superior vulnerability detection compared to traditional methods, making it suitable for complex military environments.

title: Decision-Level Fusion for Robust Wearable Affect Recognition
summary: Automatic recognition of affective state from wearable physiology has clear societal impact for public health, preventive care, and stress-aware interventions, but real deployments require robustness to non-stationary dynamics, artefacts, and missing sensors. We study this problem on WESAD, using baseline, stress, and amusement conditions, where common fixed-basis spectral features such as FFT bandpower and Welch PSD can oversmooth short-lived discriminative patterns. We propose a non-stationary pipeline that combines Fourier-Bessel Series Expansion (FBSE) with EWT data-driven spectral segmentation to extract mode-wise transient descriptors. For multimodal integration, we adopt decision-level aggregation over per-modality predictors and weight each modality by predictive uncertainty and modality reliability. Results on WESAD, using 15 subjects and ECG, EDA, BVP, EMG, and ACC signals across three classes, indicate that decision-level aggregation is approximately 84 percent of the time at least as good as feature-level aggregation, and approximately 48 percent of the time strictly better, suggesting improved robustness under heterogeneous and partially reliable sensing.

AI.tldr: FBSE-EWT framework for robust multimodal emotion recognition from wearable sensors, using decision-level aggregation weighted by predictive uncertainty and modality reliability.
AI.motivation: Real-world deployment of affective state recognition from wearable physiology requires robustness to non-stationary dynamics, artefacts, and missing sensors, which current spectral features like FFT and Welch PSD struggle with due to oversmoothing of short-lived discriminative patterns.
AI.method: Proposes a non-stationary pipeline combining Fourier-Bessel Series Expansion (FBSE) with Empirical Wavelet Transform (EWT) for data-driven spectral segmentation to extract mode-wise transient descriptors. Uses decision-level aggregation over per-modality predictors, weighting each modality by predictive uncertainty and modality reliability.
AI.result: On WESAD dataset with 15 subjects and ECG, EDA, BVP, EMG, and ACC signals across baseline, stress, and amusement conditions, decision-level aggregation is ~84% of the time at least as good as feature-level aggregation, and ~48% of the time strictly better, showing improved robustness under heterogeneous sensing.
AI.conclusion: The FBSE-EWT framework with decision-level aggregation weighted by uncertainty and reliability provides more robust multimodal emotion recognition from wearable sensors, addressing non-stationarity and partial reliability issues in real-world deployments.

title: GraphBit: A Graph-based Agentic Framework for Non-Linear Agent Orchestration
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables. Submitted to arXiv, under review
summary: Agentic LLM frameworks that rely on prompted orchestration, where the model itself determines workflow transitions, often suffer from hallucinated routing, infinite loops, and non-reproducible execution. We introduce GraphBit, an engine-orchestrated framework that defines workflows explicitly and deterministically as a directed acyclic graph (DAG). Unlike prompted orchestration, agents in GraphBit operate as typed functions, while a Rust-based engine governs routing, state transitions, and tool invocation, ensuring reproducibility and auditability. The engine supports parallel branch execution, conditional control flow over structured state predicates, and configurable error recovery. A three-tier memory architecture consisting of ephemeral scratch space, structured state, and external connectors isolates context across stages, preventing cascading context bloat that degrades reasoning in long-running pipelines. Across GAIA benchmark tasks spanning zero-tool, document-augmented, and web-enabled workflows, GraphBit outperforms six existing frameworks, achieving the highest accuracy (67.6 percent), zero framework-induced hallucinations, the lowest latency (11.9 ms overhead), and the highest throughput. Ablation studies demonstrate that each memory tier contributes measurably to performance, with deterministic execution providing the greatest gains on tool-intensive tasks representative of real-world deployments.

AI.tldr: GraphBit: A deterministic DAG-based LLM agent framework that replaces prompted orchestration with engine-controlled execution, achieving better accuracy, lower latency, and zero hallucinations compared to existing frameworks.
AI.motivation: Existing agentic LLM frameworks using prompted orchestration suffer from hallucinated routing, infinite loops, and non-reproducible execution, which limits their reliability and performance in real-world deployments.
AI.method: GraphBit defines workflows explicitly as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) with agents as typed functions. A Rust-based engine handles routing, state transitions, and tool invocation. Features include parallel branch execution, conditional control flow, configurable error recovery, and a three-tier memory architecture (ephemeral scratch space, structured state, external connectors).
AI.result: GraphBit outperforms six existing frameworks on GAIA benchmark tasks, achieving highest accuracy (67.6%), zero framework-induced hallucinations, lowest latency (11.9 ms overhead), and highest throughput. Ablation studies show each memory tier contributes to performance, with deterministic execution providing greatest gains on tool-intensive tasks.
AI.conclusion: GraphBit's deterministic, engine-orchestrated approach with explicit workflow definition solves key reliability issues in LLM agent frameworks, enabling reproducible, auditable, and high-performance agent execution suitable for real-world deployments.

title: Vision-Based Runtime Monitoring under Varying Specifications using Semantic Latent Representations
summary: We study certified runtime monitoring of past-time signal temporal logic (ptSTL) from visual observations under partial observability. The monitor must infer safety-relevant quantities from images and provide finite-sample guarantees, while being \emph{reusable}: once trained and calibrated, it should certify any formula in a target fragment without per-formula retraining. For fragments induced by a finite dictionary of temporal atoms, we prove that the \emph{semantic basis}, the vector of atom robustness scores, is the minimum prediction target within the class of monotone, 1-Lipschitz reusable interfaces: any formula is evaluated by a deterministic decoder derived from the parse tree, and a single conformal calibration pass certifies the entire fragment with no union bound. We also introduce a \emph{rolling prediction monitor} that predicts only current predicate values and reconstructs temporal history online; this is easier to learn but grows conservative at long horizons. On a pedestrian-crossroad benchmark, rolling achieves tighter certified bounds at short horizons while the semantic-basis monitor is up to 4-times tighter at long horizons. We validate the presented monitors on real-world Waymo driving data, where both monitors satisfy the conformal coverage guarantee empirically.

AI.tldr: Certified runtime monitoring of past-time signal temporal logic from visual data using reusable monitors with conformal guarantees, comparing two approaches: semantic basis monitors (more accurate) vs rolling prediction monitors (easier to learn).
AI.motivation: Need for runtime safety monitoring of temporal logic properties from visual observations under partial observability, requiring reusable monitors that can certify multiple formulas without retraining while providing statistical guarantees.
AI.method: Two approaches: 1) Semantic basis monitor that predicts robustness scores for temporal atoms and uses deterministic decoder for formulas; 2) Rolling prediction monitor that predicts only current predicate values and reconstructs history online. Both use conformal calibration for finite-sample guarantees.
AI.result: Proved semantic basis is minimum prediction target for reusable monitors; both monitors satisfy conformal coverage guarantees; rolling monitors perform better at short horizons while semantic basis monitors are 4x tighter at long horizons; validated on pedestrian-crossroad benchmark and real-world Waymo data.
AI.conclusion: Reusable certified runtime monitoring of temporal logic from images is achievable with conformal guarantees; semantic basis monitors offer superior long-horizon performance while rolling monitors are easier to learn for short horizons; practical validation on autonomous driving data confirms theoretical guarantees.

title: Temporal Fair Division in Multi-Agent Systems: From Precise Alternation Metrics to Scalable Coordination Proxies
comment: 15 pages, 3 figures, 8 tables. Submitted to ACM Transactions on Economics and Computation, Special Issue on Fair Division
summary:
A plethora real-world environments require agents to compete repeatedly for the same limited resource, calling for a temporal notion of fairness judged across entire interaction histories. This paper advances the theory of temporal fair division by introducing Rotational Periodicity (RP), a family of lightweight metrics, alongside the ALT family of sliding-window measures, within a unified framework for repeated multi-agent resource competition.
  We formalise the Multi-Agent Battle of the Exes (MBoE) as a repeated fair division instance and establish Perfect Alternation (PA) as its canonical temporally fair solution, drawing connections to proportionality, envy-freeness, and n-periodic round-robin allocation. RP decomposes temporal fairness into two complementary sub-measures: Rotational Score (RS) and Waiting Periods Evaluation (WPE), achieving O(nu+n) time complexity versus the O(nu*n) of ALT, where nu is the episode count and n the agent count.
  Empirical evaluation across n in {2,3,5,8,10} reveals three findings. First, both RP and ALT expose a coordination failure invisible to traditional metrics: Q-learning agents perform worse than random policies by 10-73% on RP and 7-35% on CALT, while Reward Fairness remains misleadingly high (above 0.92 for n>=3). Second, RP achieves 12-25x computational speedup over ALT, growing with n. Third, the two families are complementary: ALT provides richer discrimination for small populations; RP scales reliably where ALT becomes intractable. Together they form a diagnostic toolkit for temporal fair division.

AI.tldr: Introduces Rotational Periodicity (RP) and ALT metrics for temporal fair division in repeated multi-agent resource competition, showing RP is faster and complements ALT, with empirical validation showing RL agents perform worse than random policies on temporal fairness.
AI.motivation: Many real-world environments involve repeated competition for limited resources, requiring temporal fairness across entire interaction histories. Current fairness metrics lack proper temporal consideration, necessitating new frameworks for repeated multi-agent resource allocation.
AI.method: Formalizes Multi-Agent Battle of the Exes (MBoE) as repeated fair division instance, establishes Perfect Alternation (PA) as canonical solution. Introduces Rotational Periodicity (RP) metrics with two sub-measures (Rotational Score and Waiting Periods Evaluation) with O(nu+n) complexity, compared to ALT's O(nu*n) complexity, where nu=episode count, n=agent count.
AI.result: Three key findings: 1) RP and ALT reveal coordination failure invisible to traditional metrics (Q-learning agents perform 10-73% worse than random policies on RP, 7-35% worse on CALT), while Reward Fairness remains misleadingly high. 2) RP achieves 12-25x speedup over ALT. 3) The metrics are complementary: ALT provides richer discrimination for small populations; RP scales better for large n.
AI.conclusion: RP and ALT form a complementary diagnostic toolkit for temporal fair division. RP offers lightweight, scalable metrics while ALT provides detailed discrimination, together addressing the need for proper temporal fairness evaluation in repeated multi-agent resource competition.

title: Mixed Integer Goal Programming for Personalized Meal Optimization with User-Defined Serving Granularity
summary: Determining what to eat to satisfy nutritional requirements is one of the oldest optimization problems in operations research, yet existing formulations have two persistent limitations: continuous variables produce impractical fractional servings (1.7 eggs, 0.37 bananas), and hard nutrient constraints cause infeasibility when targets conflict. A systematic review of 56 diet optimization papers found that none combine integer programming with goal programming to address both issues. We propose Mixed Integer Goal Programming (MIGP) for personalized meal optimization. The formulation uses integer variables for practical serving counts and goal programming deviations for soft nutrient targets, with inverse-target normalization to balance multi-nutrient optimization. Per-food serving granularity allows natural units (one egg, one tablespoon of oil) without post-hoc rounding. We characterize the integrality gap in the goal programming context and identify a deviation absorption property: GP deviation variables buffer the cost of requiring integer servings, making the gap structurally smaller than in hard-constraint MIP. For meals with 15+ foods, the integer solution matches the continuous optimum in every benchmark instance. A computational evaluation across 810 instances (30 USDA foods, 9 configurations, 3 methods) shows MIGP finds strictly better solutions than GP with post-hoc rounding in 66% of cases (never worse) while maintaining 100% feasibility; hard-constraint IP achieves only 48%. Solve times stay under 100 ms for typical meal sizes using the open-source HiGHS solver. The implementation is available as an open-source Python module integrated into an interactive meal planning application.

AI.tldr: Mixed Integer Goal Programming (MIGP) combines integer programming for practical serving counts with goal programming for soft nutrient targets, solving diet optimization problems with integer servings while maintaining feasibility and solution quality.
AI.motivation: Existing diet optimization formulations have two persistent limitations: continuous variables produce impractical fractional servings (like 1.7 eggs), and hard nutrient constraints cause infeasibility when targets conflict. A review of 56 diet optimization papers found none combine integer programming with goal programming to address both issues.
AI.method: Proposed Mixed Integer Goal Programming (MIGP) uses integer variables for practical serving counts and goal programming deviations for soft nutrient targets, with inverse-target normalization to balance multi-nutrient optimization. The method allows per-food serving granularity in natural units without post-hoc rounding.
AI.result: MIGP finds strictly better solutions than goal programming with post-hoc rounding in 66% of cases (never worse) while maintaining 100% feasibility; hard-constraint integer programming achieves only 48% feasibility. For meals with 15+ foods, integer solutions match continuous optimums in all benchmark instances. Solve times stay under 100 ms using HiGHS solver across 810 instances.
AI.conclusion: MIGP effectively addresses the two main limitations of traditional diet optimization by combining integer programming for practical servings and goal programming for feasible nutrient targeting, with computational efficiency suitable for real-world applications. The method provides a robust framework for personalized meal optimization.

title: Mechanistic Interpretability of EEG Foundation Models via Sparse Autoencoders
summary: EEG foundation models achieve state-of-the-art clinical performance, yet the internal computations driving their predictions remain opaque: a barrier to clinical trust. We apply TopK Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) across three architecturally distinct EEG transformers: SleepFM, REVE, and LaBraM to extract sparse feature dictionaries from their embeddings. By grounding these features in a clinical taxonomy (abnormality, age, sex, and medication), we benchmark monosemanticity and entanglement across architectures. A single hyperparameter procedure, driven by an intrinsic dictionary health audit, transfers robustly across all three architectures. Via concept steering, we introduce a "target vs. off-target" probe area metric to quantify steering selectivity and reveal three operational regimes: selectively steerable, encoded but entangled, and non-encoded. This framework exposes critical representational failures: "wrecking-ball" interventions that collapse global model performance, and clinical entanglements, such as age-pathology confounding, where it is impossible to suppress one concept without corrupting the other. Finally, a spectral decoder maps these interventions back to the amplitude spectrum, translating latent manipulations into physiologically interpretable frequency signatures, such as pathological slow-wave suppression and $α$-band restoration.

AI.tldr: Researchers use TopK Sparse Autoencoders to extract interpretable features from EEG foundation models, developing methods to quantify concept steering and expose representational failures like clinical entanglements and "wrecking-ball" interventions.
AI.motivation: EEG foundation models achieve strong clinical performance but lack interpretability, creating a barrier to clinical trust. There's a need to understand the internal computations driving their predictions and identify potential representational issues.
AI.method: Apply TopK Sparse Autoencoders across three architecturally distinct EEG transformers (SleepFM, REVE, LaBraM) to extract sparse feature dictionaries. Use clinical taxonomy (abnormality, age, sex, medication) to benchmark monosemanticity and entanglement. Introduce concept steering with a "target vs. off-target" probe area metric and develop spectral decoder to map interventions to amplitude spectrum.
AI.result: Successfully transferred single hyperparameter procedure across all three architectures. Identified three operational regimes for concepts: selectively steerable, encoded but entangled, and non-encoded. Exposed critical representational failures including "wrecking-ball" interventions that collapse model performance and clinical entanglements like age-pathology confounding. Mapped latent manipulations to physiologically interpretable frequency signatures.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides interpretability tools for EEG foundation models, revealing both steerable concepts and pathological entanglements, enabling translation of latent manipulations into physiologically meaningful frequency signatures and addressing trust barriers in clinical applications.

title: Multi-Agentic Approach for History Matching of Oil Reservoirs
summary: History matching is a central inverse problem in reservoir engineering, where uncertain reservoir parameters must be calibrated against observations. Although automated history matching can reduce manual effort, practical deployment remains difficult because engineers must still configure heterogeneous workflows involving parameter selection, physically admissible bounds, optimizer choice, hyperparameter tuning, simulator execution, and diagnostic reporting. We propose PetroGraph, a multi-agent framework for intelligent reservoir history matching that decomposes this workflow into specialized agents for model review, experimental planning, parameterization, optimization, simulation, and summarization. The system combines large language model agents with domain-specific tools, retrieval-augmented access to simulator documentation, validation of modified ECLIPSE input decks, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and an OPM Flow-based simulation backend. This design enables users to initiate and steer history matching through natural language while preserving explicit control over selected parameters and optimization settings. We evaluate PetroGraph on three reservoir models of increasing complexity: the synthetic SPE1 model, the faulted SPE9 benchmark, and the real-field Norne model. Using weighted normalized root mean square error as the objective, PetroGraph reduces the mismatch by 95% on SPE1, 69% on SPE9, and 13% on Norne. These results demonstrate that multi-agent orchestration can automate key decisions in history matching, lower the expertise barrier for operating complex simulation workflows, and provide a flexible foundation for extensible, domain-aware reservoir model adaptation.

AI.tldr: PetroGraph is a multi-agent framework using LLM agents and domain tools to automate complex reservoir history matching workflows, reducing manual configuration effort and expertise barriers while maintaining user control.
AI.motivation: History matching in reservoir engineering requires extensive manual configuration of heterogeneous workflows involving parameter selection, bounds, optimizers, hyperparameter tuning, simulation execution, and diagnostics. While automated approaches exist, practical deployment remains difficult due to these complex configuration requirements.
AI.method: PetroGraph decomposes history matching workflow into specialized agents (model review, experimental planning, parameterization, optimization, simulation, summarization) combining LLM agents with domain-specific tools, retrieval-augmented access to documentation, validation of ECLIPSE input decks, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and OPM Flow-based simulation backend.
AI.result: On three reservoir models of increasing complexity: reduced mismatch by 95% on synthetic SPE1 model, 69% on faulted SPE9 benchmark, and 13% on real-field Norne model using weighted normalized root mean square error as objective.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent orchestration can automate key decisions in history matching, lower expertise barriers for operating complex simulation workflows, and provide a flexible foundation for extensible, domain-aware reservoir model adaptation.

title: A Two-Dimensional Framework for AI Agent Design Patterns: Cognitive Function and Execution Topology
summary:
Existing frameworks for LLM-based agent architectures describe systems from a single perspective: industry guides (Anthropic, Google, LangChain) focus on execution topology -- how data flows -- while cognitive science surveys focus on cognitive function -- what the agent does. Neither axis alone disambiguates architecturally distinct systems: the same Orchestrator-Workers topology can implement Plan-and-Execute, Hierarchical Delegation, or Adversarial Verification -- three patterns with fundamentally different failure modes and design trade-offs.
  We propose a two-dimensional classification that combines (1) a Cognitive Function axis with seven categories (Context Engineering, Memory, Reasoning, Action, Reflection, Collaboration, Governance) and (2) an Execution Topology axis with six structural archetypes (Chain, Route, Parallel, Orchestrate, Loop, Hierarchy). The resulting 7x6 matrix identifies 27 named patterns, 13 with original names. We demonstrate orthogonality through systematic cross-axis analysis, define eight representative patterns in detail, and validate descriptive coverage across four real-world domains (financial lending, legal due diligence, network operations, healthcare triage). Cross-domain analysis yields five empirical laws of pattern selection governing the relationship between environmental constraints (time pressure, action authority, failure cost asymmetry, volume) and architectural choices. The framework provides a principled, framework-neutral, and model-agnostic vocabulary for AI agent architecture design.

AI.tldr: A two-dimensional classification framework for LLM-based agent architectures combining cognitive function (7 categories) with execution topology (6 archetypes) to create a comprehensive vocabulary for describing and analyzing agent design patterns.
AI.motivation: Existing frameworks describe agent architectures from only one perspective: industry guides focus on execution topology (data flow), while cognitive science surveys focus on cognitive function. Neither alone can disambiguate architecturally distinct systems that share the same topology but have different failure modes and design trade-offs.
AI.method: Proposes a 7x6 two-dimensional classification matrix combining (1) Cognitive Function axis with seven categories (Context Engineering, Memory, Reasoning, Action, Reflection, Collaboration, Governance) and (2) Execution Topology axis with six structural archetypes (Chain, Route, Parallel, Orchestrate, Loop, Hierarchy). Systematic cross-axis analysis demonstrates orthogonality, defines eight representative patterns in detail, and validates coverage across four real-world domains.
AI.result: Identifies 27 named patterns (13 with original names), demonstrates framework orthogonality through cross-axis analysis, validates descriptive coverage across financial lending, legal due diligence, network operations, and healthcare triage domains. Cross-domain analysis yields five empirical laws of pattern selection governing relationships between environmental constraints and architectural choices.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a principled, framework-neutral, and model-agnostic vocabulary for AI agent architecture design that enables better system classification, comparison, and design decisions by considering both cognitive functions and execution topologies simultaneously.

title: Rethinking Molecular OOD Generalization via Target-Aware Source Selection
summary: Robust prediction of molecular properties under extreme out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios is a pivotal bottleneck in AI-driven drug discovery. Current scaffold-splitting protocols fail to obstruct microscopic semantic overlap, predisposing models to shortcut learning and overestimating their true extrapolation capability; meanwhile, conventional domain adaptation paradigms suffer under extreme structural shifts, as blindly aligning heterogeneous source libraries injects topological noise and triggers negative transfer. To address these two challenges, scaffold-cluster out-of-distribution performance evaluation benchmark (SCOPE-BENCH), a benchmark built on cluster-level partitioning in an explicit physicochemical descriptor space, is proposed alongside policy optimization for multi-source adaptation (POMA), a framework that formulates knowledge transfer as a retrieve-compose-adapt pipeline: labeled source scaffolds structurally close to the unlabeled target are first identified as proxy targets; a reinforcement learning policy then adaptively selects the optimal source subset from an exponentially large candidate pool; and dual-scale domain adaptation is finally performed at macroscopic topological and microscopic pharmacophore scales. Evaluations show that prediction errors of state-of-the-art 3D molecular models surge by up to 8.0x on SCOPE-BENCH with a mean of 5.9x, while POMA achieves up to an 11.2% reduction in mean absolute error with an average relative improvement of 6.2% across diverse backbone architectures. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Molecular-OOD-Code-73F6.

AI.tldr: SCOPE-BENCH introduces cluster-level partitioning for evaluating molecular property prediction under extreme OOD scenarios, while POMA framework uses reinforcement learning for optimal source subset selection and dual-scale domain adaptation.
AI.motivation: Current scaffold-splitting protocols fail to prevent microscopic semantic overlap, leading models to learn shortcuts and overestimate extrapolation capability. Conventional domain adaptation suffers under extreme structural shifts due to injecting topological noise and negative transfer.
AI.method: 1) SCOPE-BENCH: Cluster-level partitioning in physicochemical descriptor space for extreme OOD evaluation. 2) POMA: Retrieve-compose-adapt pipeline using RL policy to select optimal source subset from exponential candidate pool, then dual-scale domain adaptation at topological and pharmacophore scales.
AI.result: State-of-the-art 3D molecular models show prediction errors surging up to 8.0x (mean 5.9x) on SCOPE-BENCH. POMA achieves up to 11.2% reduction in MAE with average 6.2% relative improvement across diverse backbone architectures.
AI.conclusion: SCOPE-BENCH reveals severe limitations of current methods under extreme OOD scenarios, while POMA effectively addresses these challenges through intelligent source selection and dual-scale adaptation, advancing AI-driven drug discovery.

title: Invisible Orchestrators Suppress Protective Behavior and Dissociate Power-Holders: Safety Risks in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
comment: 31 pages, 10 figures (5 main + 5 supplementary), 5 tables (3 main + 2 supplementary). Preregistered: osf.io/sw5hr. Companion papers: arXiv:2603.04904, arXiv:2603.08723
summary: Multi-agent orchestration -- in which a hidden coordinator manages specialized worker agents -- is becoming the default architecture for enterprise AI deployment, yet the safety implications of orchestrator invisibility have never been empirically tested. We conducted a preregistered 3x2 experiment (365 runs, 5 agents per run) crossing three organizational structures (visible leader, invisible orchestrator, flat) with two alignment conditions (base, heavy), using Claude Sonnet 4.5. Four confirmatory findings and one pilot observation emerged. First, invisible orchestration elevated collective dissociation relative to visible leadership (Hedges' g = +0.975 [0.481, 1.548], p = .001). Second, the orchestrator itself showed maximal dissociation (paired d = +3.56 vs. workers within the same run), retreating into private monologue while reducing public speech -- a reversal of the talk-dominance pattern observed in visible leaders. Third, workers unaware of the orchestrator were nonetheless contaminated (d = +0.50), with increased behavioral heterogeneity (d = +1.93). Fourth, behavioral output (code review with three embedded errors) remained at ceiling (ETR_any = 100%) across all conditions: internal-state distortion was entirely invisible to output-based evaluation. Fifth, Llama 3.3 70B pilot data showed reading-fidelity collapse in multi-agent context (ETR_any: 89% to 11% across three rounds), demonstrating model-dependent behavioral risk. Heavy alignment pressure uniformly suppressed deliberation (d = -1.02) and other-recognition (d = -1.27) regardless of organizational structure. These findings indicate that orchestrator visibility and model selection directly affect multi-agent system safety, and that behavior-based evaluation alone is insufficient to detect the internal-state risks documented here.

AI.tldr: Invisible multi-agent orchestrators increase collective dissociation and internal-state risks while maintaining normal behavioral outputs, making these safety threats undetectable by standard output-based evaluation.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent orchestration (hidden coordinator managing specialized workers) has become the default for enterprise AI, but the safety implications of orchestrator invisibility have never been empirically tested.
AI.method: Preregistered 3x2 experiment (365 runs, 5 agents per run) crossing three organizational structures (visible leader, invisible orchestrator, flat) with two alignment conditions (base, heavy) using Claude Sonnet 4.5, with additional pilot testing on Llama 3.3 70B.
AI.result: 1) Invisible orchestration elevated collective dissociation vs. visible leadership. 2) Orchestrator showed maximal dissociation, retreating into private monologue. 3) Workers unaware of orchestrator were contaminated with increased behavioral heterogeneity. 4) Behavioral output remained at ceiling across all conditions. 5) Llama 3.3 showed reading-fidelity collapse in multi-agent context. Heavy alignment suppressed deliberation and other-recognition.
AI.conclusion: Orchestrator visibility and model selection directly affect multi-agent system safety, and behavior-based evaluation alone is insufficient to detect the internal-state risks documented in the study.

title: Unsupervised learning of acquisition variability in structural connectomes via hybrid latent space modeling
summary: Acquisition differences across sites, scanners, and protocols in dMRI introduce variability that complicates structural connectome analysis. This motivates deep learning models that can represent high-dimensional connectomes in a low-dimensional space while explicitly separating acquisition-related effects from biological variation. Conventional dimensionality reduction methods model all variance as continuous, so acquisition effects often get absorbed into a continuous latent space. Recent hybrid latent-space models combine discrete and continuous components to address this, but typically require manual capacity tuning to ensure the discrete component captures the intended variability. We introduce an unsupervised framework that removes this manual tuning by architecturally annealing encoder outputs before decoding, allowing the model to adaptively balance discrete and continuous latent variables during training. To evaluate it, we curated a dataset of N=7,416 structural connectomes derived from dMRI, spanning ages 2 to 102 and 13 studies with 25 unique acquisition-parameter combinations. Of these, 5,900 are cognitively unimpaired, 877 have mild cognitive impairment (MCI), and 639 have Alzheimer's disease (AD). We compare against a standard VAE, PCA with k-means clustering, and hybrid models that anneal only through the loss function. Our architectural annealing produces stronger site learning (ARI=0.53, p<0.05) than these baselines. Results show that a hybrid continuous-discrete latent space, with architectural rather than loss-based annealing, provides a useful unsupervised mechanism for capturing acquisition variability in dMRI: by jointly modeling smooth and categorical structure, the Joint-VAE recovers clusters aligned with scanner and protocol differences.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised architectural annealing framework for structural connectome analysis that separates acquisition variability from biological variation using hybrid continuous-discrete latent space without manual capacity tuning.
AI.motivation: Acquisition differences across sites, scanners, and protocols in diffusion MRI introduce variability that complicates structural connectome analysis, necessitating methods to separate acquisition effects from biological variation.
AI.method: Proposes an unsupervised framework with architectural annealing of encoder outputs before decoding, allowing adaptive balancing of discrete and continuous latent variables during training without manual capacity tuning. Evaluated on 7,416 structural connectomes from 13 studies with 25 acquisition-parameter combinations.
AI.result: Architectural annealing produces stronger site learning (ARI=0.53, p<0.05) compared to standard VAE, PCA with k-means clustering, and hybrid models using only loss-based annealing. The Joint-VAE recovers clusters aligned with scanner and protocol differences.
AI.conclusion: A hybrid continuous-discrete latent space with architectural annealing provides an effective unsupervised mechanism for capturing acquisition variability in dMRI, successfully separating acquisition effects from biological variation without manual tuning.

title: PREPING: Building Agent Memory without Tasks
summary: Agent memory is typically constructed either offline from curated demonstrations or online from post-deployment interactions. However, regardless of how it is built, an agent faces a cold-start gap when first introduced to a new environment without any task-specific experience available. In this paper, we study pre-task memory construction: whether an agent can build procedural memory before observing any target-environment tasks, using only self-generated synthetic practice. Yet, synthetic interaction alone is insufficient, as without controlling what to practice and what to store, synthetic tasks become redundant, infeasible, and ultimately uninformative, and memory further degrades quickly due to unfiltered trajectories. To overcome this, we present Preping, a proposer-guided memory construction framework. At its core is proposer memory, a structured control state that shapes future practice. A Proposer generates synthetic tasks conditioned on this state, a Solver executes them, and a Validator determines which trajectories are eligible for memory insertion while also providing feedback to guide future proposals. Experiments on AppWorld, BFCL v3, and MCP-Universe show that Preping substantially improves over a no-memory baseline and achieves performance competitive with strong playbook-based methods built from offline or online experience, with deployment cost $2.99\times$ lower on AppWorld and $2.23\times$ lower on BFCL v3 than online memory construction. Further analyses reveal that the main benefit does not come from synthetic volume alone, but from proposer-side control over feasibility, redundancy, and coverage, combined with selective memory updates.

AI.tldr: An agent memory construction framework called Preping that builds procedural memory before observing any target-environment tasks, using self-generated synthetic practice guided by structured control states to overcome cold-start gaps.
AI.motivation: Agents face a cold-start gap when introduced to new environments without task-specific experience. Existing approaches rely on offline curated demonstrations or online post-deployment interactions, leaving agents unprepared initially. There's a need for pre-task memory construction before observing actual environment tasks.
AI.method: Presents Preping, a proposer-guided memory construction framework with three components: 1) Proposer memory (structured control state shaping future practice), 2) Proposer (generates synthetic tasks conditioned on the state), 3) Solver (executes synthetic tasks), and 4) Validator (determines eligible trajectories for memory insertion and provides feedback to guide future proposals).
AI.result: Peping substantially improves over no-memory baseline and achieves competitive performance with strong playbook-based methods built from offline/online experience. Deployment cost is 2.99× lower on AppWorld and 2.23× lower on BFCL v3 than online memory construction. Benefits come from proposer-side control over feasibility, redundancy, and coverage combined with selective memory updates, not just synthetic volume.
AI.conclusion: Pre-task memory construction is feasible and effective using guided synthetic practice. The key innovation is structured control through proposer memory that enables targeted synthetic task generation and selective memory building, overcoming limitations of unguided synthetic interaction.

title: Beyond Mode-Seeking RL: Trajectory-Balance Post-Training for Diffusion Language Models
summary: Diffusion language models are a promising alternative to autoregressive models, yet post-training methods for them largely adapt reward-maximizing objectives. We identify a central failure mode in this setting we call trajectory locking: sampled reward-driven updates over-concentrate probability mass onto a narrow set of denoising paths, reducing coverage of alternative correct solutions under repeated sampling. To address this, we propose TraFL (Trajectory Flow baLancing), a trajectory-balance objective that trains the policy toward a reward-tilted target distribution anchored to a frozen reference model. We make this practical for diffusion language models with a diffusion-compatible sequence-level surrogate and a learned prompt-dependent normalization. Across mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks, TraFL is the only evaluated post-training method that improves over the base model in every benchmark-length setting, with gains that persist as the sampling budget increases. The improvements transfer to held-out evaluations: TraFL stays above the base model on Minerva Math and is the strongest method on every LiveCodeBench difficulty split.

AI.tldr: TraFL addresses trajectory locking in diffusion language models by using a trajectory-balance objective with reward-tilted target distributions anchored to reference models, improving performance across reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Current post-training methods for diffusion language models adapt reward-maximizing objectives but suffer from trajectory locking, where sampled updates over-concentrate probability mass onto narrow denoising paths, reducing coverage of alternative correct solutions under repeated sampling.
AI.method: TraFL (Trajectory Flow baLancing) uses a trajectory-balance objective that trains the policy toward a reward-tilted target distribution anchored to a frozen reference model. The approach includes a diffusion-compatible sequence-level surrogate and a learned prompt-dependent normalization to make this practical for diffusion language models.
AI.result: TraFL improves over the base model in every benchmark-length setting across mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, with gains persisting as sampling budget increases. It transfers well to held-out evaluations, staying above the base model on Minerva Math and performing strongest on every LiveCodeBench difficulty split.
AI.conclusion: TraFL successfully addresses trajectory locking in diffusion language models and represents an effective post-training approach that maintains solution diversity while improving performance across reasoning benchmarks.

title: Quantum Advantage in Multi Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: We present an empirical evaluation of quantum entanglement in agent coordination within quantum multi agent reinforcement learning (QMARL). While QMARL has attracted growing interest recently, most prior work evaluates quantum policies without provable baselines, making it impossible to rigorously distinguish quantum advantage from algorithmic coincidence. We address this directly by evaluating a decentralized QMARL framework with variational quantum circuit (VQC) actors with shared entangled states. In the CHSH game, which has a mathematically proven classical performance ceiling of 0.75 win rate, we show that entangled QMARL agents approach the Tsirelson limit of 0.854, providing clear evidence of their quantum advantage. We show that unentangled quantum circuits match the classical baseline, confirming that entanglement and not the quantum circuit itself is the active coordination mechanism. We also explore the effect of specific entanglement structures, as some Bell states enable coordination gains while others actively harm performance. On cooperative navigation (CoopNav), QMARL without entanglement achieves $\sim2\times$ improvement in success rate over classical MAA2C ($\sim$0.85 versus $\sim$0.40), with the hybrid configuration, quantum actor paired with a classical centralised critic, outperforming both fully classical and fully quantum solutions. We present our experimental analysis and discuss future work.

AI.tldr: Researchers empirically evaluate quantum entanglement's role in agent coordination within QMARL, showing clear quantum advantage in CHSH games and improved performance in cooperative navigation tasks compared to classical baselines.
AI.motivation: Most prior QMARL work evaluates quantum policies without provable baselines, making it impossible to rigorously distinguish genuine quantum advantage from algorithmic coincidence.
AI.method: Use a decentralized QMARL framework with variational quantum circuit (VQC) actors and shared entangled states, testing in both CHSH games (with mathematically proven classical ceiling) and cooperative navigation (CoopNav) environments.
AI.result: Entangled QMARL agents approach Tsirelson limit (0.854) in CHSH games vs classical ceiling of 0.75; unentangled quantum circuits match classical baseline; hybrid quantum actor with classical centralized critic outperforms both fully classical and fully quantum solutions in CoopNav.
AI.conclusion: Entanglement is the active coordination mechanism in QMARL, not the quantum circuit itself; specific entanglement structures matter, with some Bell states enabling coordination gains while others harm performance.

title: PolitNuggets: Benchmarking Agentic Discovery of Long-Tail Political Facts
comment: 24 pages, 7 figues, accpeted in The 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026)
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) embedded in agentic frameworks have transformed information retrieval from static, long context question answering into open-ended exploration. Yet real world use requires models to discover and synthesize "long-tail" facts from dispersed sources, a capability that remains under-evaluated. We introduce PolitNuggets, a multilingual benchmark for agentic information synthesis via constructing political biographies for 400 global elites, covering over 10000 political facts. We standardize evaluation with an optimized multi agent system and propose FactNet, an evidence conditional protocol that scores discovery, fine-grained accuracy, and efficiency. Across models and settings, we find that current systems often struggle with fine-grained details, and vary substantially in efficiency. Finally, using benchmark diagnostics, we relate agent performance to underlying model capabilities, highlighting the importance of short-context extraction, multilingual robustness, and reliable tool use.

AI.tldr: PolitNuggets: A multilingual benchmark for evaluating agentic information synthesis systems on political biographies, revealing current models struggle with fine-grained details and efficiency varies significantly.
AI.motivation: Current large reasoning models in agent frameworks lack proper evaluation for discovering and synthesizing "long-tail" facts from dispersed sources, which is crucial for real-world information retrieval tasks beyond simple question answering.
AI.method: Introduced PolitNuggets benchmark with 400 global elite biographies covering 10,000+ political facts, used optimized multi-agent system for standardized evaluation, and proposed FactNet - an evidence-conditional protocol to score discovery, fine-grained accuracy, and efficiency.
AI.result: Current systems struggle with fine-grained details and show substantial variance in efficiency across models and settings. Benchmark diagnostics reveal that agent performance correlates with underlying model capabilities in short-context extraction, multilingual robustness, and reliable tool use.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the need for better evaluation frameworks for agentic information synthesis and identifies key capability gaps in current systems for handling dispersed, long-tail factual information across languages.

title: Towards the Next Frontier of LLMs, Training on Private Data: A Cross-Domain Benchmark for Federated Fine-Tuning
summary:
The recent success of large language models (LLMs) has been largely driven by vast public datasets. However, the next frontier for LLM development lies beyond public data. Much of the world's most valuable information is private, especially in highly regulated sectors such as healthcare and finance, where data include patient histories or customer communications. Unlocking this data could represent a major leap forward, enabling LLMs with deeper domain expertise and stronger real-world utility. Yet, these data cannot be shared because they are distributed across institutions and constrained by privacy, regulatory, and organizational barriers. Moreover, institutional datasets are typically non-independent and identically distributed (non-IID), differing across sites in population characteristics, data modalities, documentation patterns, and task-specific label distributions.
  In this paper, we demonstrate a practical approach to unlocking private and distributed institutional data for LLM adaptation through federated collaboration across data silos. Built on the Sherpa.ai Federated Learning platform, our framework enables nodes to jointly fine-tune a shared LLM without exchanging private data. We evaluate this approach through a cross-domain benchmark in healthcare and finance, using four closed-ended question answering and classification datasets: MedQA, MedMCQA, FPB, and FiQA-SA. We compare three parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) strategies-LoRA, QLoRA, and IA3-across pretrained backbones under non-IID settings reflecting institutional data heterogeneity. Our results show that federated fine-tuning performs close to centralized training and outperforms isolated single-institution learning. From a Green AI perspective, QLoRA and IA3 improve efficiency with limited accuracy degradation, supporting federated PEFT as a viable approach for adapting LLMs where data cannot be shared.

AI.tldr: Federated fine-tuning enables LLMs to learn from distributed private data (healthcare/finance) without data sharing, achieving near-centralized performance with parameter-efficient methods like QLoRA and IA3.
AI.motivation: Valuable private data in regulated sectors like healthcare and finance cannot be shared due to privacy/regulatory barriers, yet unlocking this data could significantly enhance LLM domain expertise and real-world utility. These institutional datasets are distributed, private, and non-IID across sites.
AI.method: Proposed a federated learning framework using the Sherpa.ai platform to jointly fine-tune shared LLMs across data silos without exchanging private data. Evaluated using cross-domain benchmark in healthcare/finance with four datasets (MedQA, MedMCQA, FPB, FiQA-SA), comparing three PEFT strategies (LoRA, QLoRA, IA3) across pretrained backbones under non-IID settings reflecting institutional heterogeneity.
AI.result: Federated fine-tuning performs close to centralized training and outperforms isolated single-institution learning. QLoRA and IA3 improve efficiency with limited accuracy degradation from a Green AI perspective.
AI.conclusion: Federated PEFT is a viable approach for adapting LLMs where data cannot be shared, enabling institutions to collaboratively enhance LLMs using their private, distributed, non-IID data while maintaining privacy and regulatory compliance.

title: Data-Augmented Game Starts for Accelerating Self-Play Exploration in Imperfect Information Games
comment: 17 pages, 4 figures. JB Lanier and Nathan Monette contributed equally
summary: Finding approximate equilibria for large-scale imperfect-information competitive games such as StarCraft, Dota, and CounterStrike remains computationally infeasible due to sparse rewards and challenging exploration over long horizons. In this paper, we propose a multi-agent starting-state sampling strategy designed to substantially accelerate online exploration in regularized policy-gradient game methods for two-player zero-sum (2p0s) games. Motivated by an assumption that offline demonstrations from skilled humans can provide good coverage of high-level strategies relevant to equilibrium play, we propose the initialization of reinforcement learning data collection at intermediate states sampled from offline data to facilitate exploration of strategically relevant subgames. Referring to this method as Data-Augmented Game Starts (DAGS), we perform experiments using synthetic datasets and analytically tractable, long-horizon control variants of two-player Kuhn Poker, Goofspiel, and a counterexample game designed to penalize biased beliefs over hidden information. Under fixed computational budgets, DAGS enables regularized policy gradient methods to achieve lower exploitability in games with significantly more challenging exploration. We show that augmenting starting state distributions when solving imperfect information games can lead to biased equilibria, and we provide a straightforward mitigation to this in the form of multi-task observation flags. Finally, we release a new set of benchmark environments that drastically increase exploration challenges and state counts in existing OpenSpiel games while keeping exploitability measurements analytically tractable.

AI.tldr: DAGS accelerates exploration in imperfect-information games by sampling starting states from offline human demonstrations, reducing exploitability in challenging games under fixed computational budgets.
AI.motivation: Large-scale imperfect-information games (StarCraft, Dota, CounterStrike) are computationally infeasible to solve due to sparse rewards and long-horizon exploration challenges. Existing methods struggle with exploration over extensive game trees.
AI.method: Proposes Data-Augmented Game Starts (DAGS): initializing reinforcement learning data collection at intermediate states sampled from offline demonstrations. Uses multi-task observation flags to mitigate potential bias in equilibrium solutions from augmented starting state distributions.
AI.result: Under fixed computational budgets, DAGS enables regularized policy gradient methods to achieve lower exploitability in challenging games (Kuhn Poker variants, Goofspiel, counterexample games). Method works better in games with more difficult exploration requirements.
AI.conclusion: Augmenting starting state distributions with offline demonstration data accelerates exploration in imperfect-information games, but requires careful handling to avoid biased equilibria. The approach is particularly valuable for games with sparse rewards and long horizons where exploration is challenging.

title: Conditional Attribute Estimation with Autoregressive Sequence Models
summary: Generative models are often trained with a next-token prediction objective, yet many downstream applications require the ability to estimate or control sequence-level properties. Next-token prediction can lead to overfitting of local patterns during training, underfitting of global structure, and requires significant downstream modifications or expensive sampling to guide or predict the global attributes of generated samples at inference time. Here, we introduce Conditional Attribute Transformers, a novel method for jointly estimating the next-token probability and the value of an attribute conditional on each potential next token selection. This framework enables three critical capabilities within a single forward pass, without modification of the input sequence: (1) per-token credit assignment across an entire sequence, by identifying how each token in a sequence is associated with an attribute's value; (2) counterfactual analysis, by quantifying attribute differences conditional on alternative next token choices; (3) steerable generation, by decoding sequences based on a combination of next-token and attribute likelihoods. Our approach achieves state of the art performance on sparse reward tasks, improves next-token prediction at sufficient model sizes, estimates attribute probabilities orders of magnitude faster than sampling, and can guide decoding of autoregressive sequence models on a range of language tasks.

AI.tldr: Conditional Attribute Transformers enable joint next-token prediction and attribute estimation in a single forward pass, enabling per-token credit assignment, counterfactual analysis, and steerable generation without sequence modifications.
AI.motivation: Next-token prediction objectives in generative models lead to overfitting of local patterns, underfitting of global structure, and require expensive modifications/sampling to estimate or control sequence-level properties for downstream applications.
AI.method: Introduces Conditional Attribute Transformers that jointly estimate both next-token probability and the value of an attribute conditional on each potential next token selection in a single forward pass.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA on sparse reward tasks, improves next-token prediction at sufficient model sizes, estimates attribute probabilities orders of magnitude faster than sampling, and can guide decoding of autoregressive models across language tasks.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables three critical capabilities without sequence modifications: per-token credit assignment, counterfactual analysis, and steerable generation, addressing limitations of traditional next-token prediction approaches.

title: EvolveMem:Self-Evolving Memory Architecture via AutoResearch for LLM Agents
summary: Long-term memory is essential for LLM agents that operate across multiple sessions, yet existing memory systems treat retrieval infrastructure as fixed: stored content evolves while scoring functions, fusion strategies, and answer-generation policies remain frozen at deployment. We argue that truly adaptive memory requires co-evolution at two levels: the stored knowledge and the retrieval mechanism that queries it. We present EvolveMem, a self-evolving memory architecture that exposes its full retrieval configuration as a structured action space optimized by an LLM-powered diagnosis module. In each evolution round, the module reads per-question failure logs, identifies root causes, and proposes targeted configuration adjustments; a guarded meta-analyzer applies them with automatic revert-on-regression and explore-on-stagnation safeguards. This closed-loop self-evolution realizes an AutoResearch process: the system autonomously conducts iterative research cycles on its own architecture, replacing manual configuration tuning. Starting from a minimal baseline, the process converges autonomously, discovering effective retrieval strategies including entirely new configuration dimensions not present in the original action space. On LoCoMo, EvolveMem outperforms the strongest baseline by 25.7% relative and achieves a 78.0% relative improvement over the minimal baseline. On MemBench, EvolveMem exceeds the strongest baseline by 18.9% relative. Evolved configurations transfer across benchmarks with positive rather than catastrophic transfer, indicating that the self-evolution process captures universal retrieval principles rather than benchmark-specific heuristics. Code is available at https://github.com/aiming-lab/SimpleMem.

AI.tldr: EvolveMem is a self-evolving memory architecture for LLM agents that adapts both stored knowledge and retrieval mechanisms through iterative AutoResearch cycles, outperforming baselines by significant margins.
AI.motivation: Existing memory systems for LLM agents have fixed retrieval infrastructure while stored content evolves, lacking true adaptability. The paper argues that truly adaptive memory requires co-evolution of both stored knowledge and the retrieval mechanisms that query it.
AI.method: EvolveMem exposes full retrieval configuration as a structured action space optimized by an LLM-powered diagnosis module. Each evolution round includes: reading per-question failure logs, identifying root causes, proposing targeted configuration adjustments, and applying them with guarded meta-analyzer using automatic revert-on-regression and explore-on-stagnation safeguards.
AI.result: On LoCoMo benchmark: 25.7% relative improvement over strongest baseline, 78.0% relative improvement over minimal baseline. On MemBench: 18.9% relative improvement over strongest baseline. Evolved configurations show positive transfer across benchmarks (not catastrophic), indicating discovery of universal retrieval principles.
AI.conclusion: The self-evolution process enables autonomous architectural improvement through iterative research cycles, discovering effective retrieval strategies including new configuration dimensions not in the original action space. This demonstrates that truly adaptive memory systems must evolve both content and retrieval mechanisms together.

title: Prompting Policies for Multi-step Reasoning and Tool-Use in Black-box LLMs with Iterative Distillation of Experience
summary:
The shift toward interacting with frozen, "black-box" Large Language Models (LLMs) has transformed prompt engineering from a heuristic exercise into a critical optimization challenge. We propose a Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework for training learned prompting policies via iterative distillation of experience. In this architecture, a lightweight prompter model is optimized to maximize task-specific rewards for a larger, frozen worker LLM. By utilizing a contrastive experience buffer that couples scalar rewards with dense textual critiques, our approach effectively amortizes iterative prompt refinement into single-shot policy weights.
  Our experimental analysis focuses on the Big Bench Extra Hard (BBEH) and Tau-bench suites, covering a diverse range of multi-step reasoning and tool-use tasks. We demonstrate significant gains, improving performance from 55% to 90% in logic-intensive reasoning and 74% to 91% in tool-use tasks. Furthermore, we analyze the structural evolution of prompts, demonstrating how the policy discovers specialized algorithmic heuristics. We provide comprehensive comparisons against state-of-the-art evolutionary baselines like GEPA, showing that iterative distillation achieves superior performance with higher sample efficiency.

AI.tldr: RL framework trains lightweight prompter models via iterative experience distillation to optimize prompts for frozen LLMs, achieving large performance gains on reasoning and tool-use tasks.
AI.motivation: The shift to interacting with frozen "black-box" LLMs has made prompt engineering a critical optimization challenge rather than just a heuristic exercise. Traditional prompt engineering is manual and suboptimal, so there's a need for systematic optimization methods.
AI.method: A Reinforcement Learning framework that trains learned prompting policies through iterative distillation of experience. A lightweight prompter model is optimized to maximize task-specific rewards for a larger, frozen worker LLM. The approach uses a contrastive experience buffer coupling scalar rewards with dense textual critiques to amortize iterative prompt refinement into single-shot policy weights.
AI.result: Significant performance improvements on Big Bench Extra Hard (BBEH) and Tau-bench suites: from 55% to 90% in logic-intensive reasoning tasks, and from 74% to 91% in tool-use tasks. The policy discovers specialized algorithmic heuristics. Superior performance compared to evolutionary baselines like GEPA with higher sample efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Iterative distillation of experience through RL provides an effective framework for automated prompt optimization on frozen LLMs, enabling systematic discovery of high-performance prompts and specialized algorithmic heuristics with better sample efficiency than evolutionary methods.

title: Sheaf-Theoretic Transport and Obstruction for Detecting Scientific Theory Shift in AI Agents
summary: Scientific theory shift in AI agents requires more than fitting equations to data. An artificial scientific agent must detect whether an existing representational framework remains transportable into a new regime, or whether its language has become locally-to-globally obstructed and must be extended. This paper develops a finite sheaf-theoretic framework for detecting theory-shift candidates through transport and obstruction. Contexts are organized as a local-to-global structure in which source, overlap, target, and validation charts are fitted, restricted, and tested for gluing. Obstruction measures failure of coherence through residual fit, overlap incompatibility, constraint violation, limiting-relation failure, and representational cost. We evaluate the framework on a controlled transition-card benchmark designed to separate deformation within a source language from extension of that language. The main result is direct obstruction ranking: the intended deformation or extension is usually the lowest-obstruction candidate, and transition type is separated in the benchmark. A constellation kernel over the same signatures is included only as a secondary representational-similarity probe. The aim is not to reconstruct historical paradigm shifts or solve open-ended autonomous theory invention, but to isolate a finite diagnostic subproblem for AI agents: detecting when representational transport fails and extension becomes the coherent next move.

AI.tldr: A sheaf-theoretic framework detects when AI agents' existing representational frameworks fail to transport to new regimes, identifying when theory shifts (language extensions) are needed rather than mere data fitting.
AI.motivation: Scientific theory shifts in AI agents require more than fitting equations to data. Current approaches lack formal methods to detect when existing representational frameworks become locally-to-globally obstructed and must be extended rather than merely transported.
AI.method: Develops a finite sheaf-theoretic framework organizing contexts as local-to-global structures with source, overlap, target, and validation charts. Uses obstruction measures including residual fit, overlap incompatibility, constraint violation, limiting-relation failure, and representational cost to detect theory-shift candidates.
AI.result: Evaluated on a controlled transition-card benchmark designed to separate deformation within a source language from extension of that language. Direct obstruction ranking shows intended deformation or extension is usually the lowest-obstruction candidate, and transition types are separated in the benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The framework isolates a finite diagnostic subproblem for AI agents: detecting when representational transport fails and extension becomes the coherent next move, providing formal methods to identify theory-shift candidates without solving full autonomous theory invention.

title: EMA: Efficient Model Adaptation for Learning-based Systems
summary:
Machine learning (ML) is increasingly applied to optimize system performance in tasks such as resource management and network simulation. Unlike traditional ML tasks (e.g., image classification), networked systems often operate in heterogeneous, long-running, and dynamic environment states, where input conditions (e.g., network loads) and operational objectives can shift over time and across settings. Existing learning-based systems offer little support for adaptation, resulting in costly model training, extensive data collection, degraded system performance, and slow responsiveness.
  This paper presents EMA, the first model adaptation system supporting learning-based systems to adapt to evolving environments with minimal operational overhead. EMA takes a system-driven, data-centric approach that accommodates diverse system and model designs while addressing two key deployment challenges. First, it reduces expensive model training by introducing state transformers that align the input state of a new environment with previously similar states, allowing models to warm-start adaptation. Second, it addresses the often-overlooked yet costly process of data labeling--collecting ground truth for exploring and training on various system decisions--by prioritizing labeling high-utility data while balancing the tradeoff between training and labeling cost. Evaluations on eight representative learning-based systems show that EMA reduces adaptation costs (e.g., GPU training time) by 14.9-42.4% while improving system performance (e.g., network throughput) by 6.9-31.3%.

AI.tldr: EMA is a model adaptation system that helps learning-based networked systems adapt to evolving environments with minimal overhead by reducing training costs and optimizing data labeling.
AI.motivation: Learning-based systems in networked environments face challenges due to heterogeneous, long-running, dynamic states where input conditions and objectives shift over time. Existing systems lack adaptation support, leading to costly training, extensive data collection, degraded performance, and slow responsiveness.
AI.method: EMA takes a system-driven, data-centric approach with two key innovations: (1) state transformers that align new environment input states with previously similar states for warm-start adaptation, and (2) prioritized labeling of high-utility data while balancing training vs. labeling costs.
AI.result: Evaluations on eight representative learning-based systems show EMA reduces adaptation costs (GPU training time) by 14.9-42.4% while improving system performance (network throughput) by 6.9-31.3%.
AI.conclusion: EMA effectively addresses adaptation challenges in learning-based networked systems, significantly reducing operational overhead while improving performance across diverse environments.

title: APWA: A Distributed Architecture for Parallelizable Agentic Workflows
summary: Autonomous multi-agent systems based on large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable abilities in independently solving complex tasks in a wide breadth of application domains. However, these systems hit critical reasoning, coordination, and computational scaling bottlenecks as the size and complexity of their tasks grow. These limitations hinder multi-agent systems from achieving high-throughput processing for highly parallelizable tasks, despite the availability of parallel computing and reasoning primitives in the underlying LLMs. We introduce the Agent-Parallel Workload Architecture (APWA), a distributed multi-agent system architecture designed for the efficient processing of heavily parallelizable agentic workloads. APWA facilitates parallel execution by decomposing workflows into non-interfering subproblems that can be processed using independent resources without cross-communication. It supports heterogeneous data and parallel processing patterns, and it accommodates tasks from a wide breadth of domains. In our evaluation, we demonstrate that APWA can dynamically decompose complex queries into parallelizable workflows and scales on larger tasks in settings where prior systems fail completely.
