Swin-Tiny showed that readout structure (spatial feature-map hierarchy) rather than architecture family determines Grad-CAM compatibility. Model-agnostic RISE compressed all families to Deletion AUC ~0.1, outperforming native methods. Faithfulness rankings reverse under different perturbation baselines. Audit results are dataset/task dependent.
AI.conclusion: Native readout is a compatibility principle, not an optimality guarantee. Explanation faithfulness depends on structural distance from model's native decision mechanism. Explanation pathways should be co-designed with model architectures based on readout structure, and deployed heatmaps should include quantitative faithfulness metrics.

title: BEAM: Binary Expert Activation Masking for Dynamic Routing in MoE
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures enhance the efficiency of large language models by activating only a subset of experts per token. However, standard MoE employs a fixed Top-K routing strategy, leading to redundant computation and suboptimal inference latency. Existing acceleration methods either require costly retraining with architectural changes or suffer from severe performance drop at high sparsity due to train-inference mismatch. To address these limitations, we propose BEAM (Binary Expert Activation Masking), a novel method that learns token-adaptive expert selection via trainable binary masks. With a straight-through estimator and an auxiliary regularization loss, BEAM induces dynamic expert sparsity through end-to-end training while maintaining model capability. We further implement an efficient custom CUDA kernel for BEAM, ensuring seamless integration with the vLLM inference framework. Experiments show that BEAM retains over 98\% of the original model's performance while reducing MoE layer FLOPs by up to 85\%, achieving up to 2.5$\times$ faster decoding and 1.4$\times$ higher throughput, demonstrating its effectiveness as a practical, plug-and-play solution for efficient MoE inference.

AI.tldr: BEAM introduces trainable binary masks for dynamic expert selection in Mixture-of-Experts models, achieving up to 85% FLOPs reduction and 2.5× faster decoding with minimal performance loss.
AI.motivation: Standard MoE models use fixed Top-K routing which leads to redundant computation and suboptimal inference latency. Existing acceleration methods either require costly retraining or suffer from severe performance degradation at high sparsity levels due to train-inference mismatch.
AI.method: BEAM learns token-adaptive expert selection via trainable binary masks. It uses a straight-through estimator and auxiliary regularization loss to induce dynamic expert sparsity through end-to-end training. An efficient custom CUDA kernel was implemented for integration with vLLM inference framework.
AI.result: BEAM retains over 98% of original model performance while reducing MoE layer FLOPs by up to 85%. It achieves up to 2.5× faster decoding and 1.4× higher throughput compared to standard MoE implementations.
AI.conclusion: BEAM provides a practical, plug-and-play solution for efficient MoE inference that maintains model capability while significantly reducing computational costs and improving inference speed through dynamic expert sparsity.

title: Dynamics of the Transformer Residual Stream: Coupling Spectral Geometry to Network Topology
summary: Large language models are remarkably capable, yet how computation propagates through their layers remains poorly understood. A growing line of work treats depth as discrete time and the residual stream as a dynamical system, where each layer's nonlinear update has a local linear description. However, previous analyses have relied on scalar summaries or approximate linearizations, leaving the full spectral geometry of trained LLMs unknown. We perform full Jacobian eigendecomposition across three production--scale LLMs and show that training installs a monotonic spectral gradient through depth -- from non-normal, rotation-dominated early layers to near--symmetric late layers -- together with a cumulative low-rank bottleneck that funnels perturbations into a small fraction of the residual stream's effective dimensions. Our experiments reveal that this gradient and the dimensional collapse are learned rather than architectural, and is largely dissolved when structured non-normality is removed. We further show that the topological positioning of graph communities predicts whether the Jacobian amplifies or suppresses them, with the sign of the coupling determined by the local operator type, a relationship absent at initialization. These results map a learned spectral geometry in LLMs that links perturbation propagation and compression to the network's functional topology.

AI.tldr: LLMs develop a learned spectral gradient through depth - from non-normal/rotating early layers to near-symmetric late layers - creating a low-rank bottleneck that funnels perturbations into a small fraction of dimensions.
AI.motivation: While large language models are highly capable, understanding how computation propagates through their layers remains poorly understood. Current analyses using scalar summaries or approximate linearizations fail to capture the full spectral geometry of trained LLMs.
AI.method: Performed full Jacobian eigendecomposition across three production-scale LLMs, analyzing the spectral geometry through depth. Also examined how spectral properties change during training and when structured non-normality is removed.
AI.result: Training installs a monotonic spectral gradient: early layers are non-normal and rotation-dominated, while late layers are near-symmetric. A cumulative low-rank bottleneck funnels perturbations into a small fraction of the residual stream's effective dimensions. This gradient is learned rather than architectural and dissolves when structured non-normality is removed. Additionally, topological positioning of graph communities predicts whether Jacobian amplifies or suppresses them.
AI.conclusion: The study maps a learned spectral geometry in LLMs that links perturbation propagation and compression to the network's functional topology, revealing how training organizes computation through depth via spectral gradients and dimensional collapse.

title: Synthesizing POMDP Policies: Sampling Meets Model-checking via Learning
comment: Paper accepted at 38th International Conference on Computer Aided Verification (CAV 2026), Lisbon, Portugal, July 2026
summary: Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) are the standard framework for decision-making under uncertainty. While sampling-based methods scale well, they lack formal correctness guarantees, making them unsuitable for safety-critical applications. Conversely, formal synthesis techniques provide correctness-by-construction but often struggle with scalability, as general POMDP synthesis is undecidable. To bridge this gap, we propose a synthesis framework that integrates sampling, automata learning, and model-checking. Inspired by Angluin's $L^*$ algorithm, our approach utilizes sampling as a membership oracle and model-checking as an equivalence oracle. This enables the synthesis of finite-state controllers with formal guarantees, provided the sampling-induced policy is regular. We establish a relative completeness result for this framework. Experimental results from our prototypical implementation demonstrate that this method successfully solves threshold-safety problems that remain challenging for existing formal synthesis tools. We believe our algorithm serves as a valuable component in a portfolio approach to tackling the inherent difficulty of POMDP synthesis problems.

AI.tldr: A synthesis framework for POMDPs that combines sampling, automata learning, and model-checking to produce finite-state controllers with formal guarantees, bridging the gap between scalability and correctness.
AI.motivation: POMDPs are standard for decision-making under uncertainty, but there's a gap between scalable sampling methods (lacking formal guarantees) and formal synthesis techniques (struggling with scalability and facing undecidability issues in general POMDP synthesis).
AI.method: Integrates sampling, automata learning, and model-checking in a framework inspired by Angluin's L* algorithm, using sampling as a membership oracle and model-checking as an equivalence oracle to synthesize finite-state controllers with formal guarantees.
AI.result: Establishes relative completeness result for the framework, and experimental results from a prototypical implementation show successful solving of threshold-safety problems that are challenging for existing formal synthesis tools.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithm serves as a valuable component in a portfolio approach to tackle the inherent difficulty of POMDP synthesis problems, successfully bridging the scalability-correctness gap for safety-critical applications.

title: TILT: Target-induced loss tilting under covariate shift
summary: We introduce and analyze Target-Induced Loss Tilting (TILT) for unsupervised domain adaptation under covariate shift. It is based on a novel objective function that decomposes the source predictor as $f+b$, fits $f+b$ on labeled source data while simultaneously penalizing the auxiliary component $b$ on unlabeled target inputs. The resulting fit $f$ is deployed as the final target predictor. At the population level, we show that this target-side penalty implicitly induces relative importance weighting at the population level, but in terms of an estimand $b^*_f$ that is self-localized to the current error, and remains uniformly bounded for any source-target pair (even those with disjoint supports). We prove a general finite-sample oracle inequality on the excess risk, and use it to give an end-to-end guarantee for training with sparse ReLU networks. Experiments on controlled regression problems and shifted CIFAR-100 distillation show that TILT improves target-domain performance over source-only training, exact importance weighting, and relative density-ratio baselines, with a stable dependence on the regularization parameter.

AI.tldr: TILT (Target-Induced Loss Tilting) is a novel unsupervised domain adaptation method that uses target-side penalty on an auxiliary component to improve target-domain performance without explicit importance weighting.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses unsupervised domain adaptation under covariate shift, where the input distribution differs between source and target domains but the conditional distribution of labels remains the same. Traditional importance weighting methods can be unstable and fail when source-target supports are disjoint.
AI.method: TILT decomposes the source predictor as f+b, fits f+b on labeled source data, and penalizes the auxiliary component b on unlabeled target inputs. The final predictor f is deployed on the target domain, with the target-side penalty implicitly inducing relative importance weighting that remains bounded even for disjoint supports.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows TILT induces self-localized importance weighting with uniform bounds. Finite-sample oracle inequality provides end-to-end guarantees for sparse ReLU networks. Experiments on controlled regression and shifted CIFAR-100 distillation show TILT outperforms source-only training, exact importance weighting, and density-ratio baselines.
AI.conclusion: TILT provides an effective approach for unsupervised domain adaptation that avoids unstable density ratio estimation, works with disjoint supports, and offers theoretical guarantees with stable regularization dependence.

title: Smooth Multi-Policy Causal Effect Estimation in Longitudinal Settings
summary: Comparative evaluation of multiple dynamic treatment policies is essential for healthcare and policy decisions, yet conventional longitudinal causal inference methods estimate each in isolation, preventing information sharing across counterfactuals. We demonstrate that this separate estimation paradigm induces a structurally uncontrolled second-order bias, inflating finite-sample variance even after standard debiasing with longitudinal targeted maximum likelihood estimation(LTMLE). To address this, we propose a policy-aware reparameterization of Iterative Conditional Expectation (ICE) Q-functions that enables joint estimation through shared representations. We implement this approach in the Policy-Encoded Q Network (PEQ-Net), an architecture centered on a shared policy encoder. The encoder is trained using kernel mean embeddings, ensuring that the learned representation space reflects population-level policy dissimilarities. After applying an LTMLE correction step, we prove this design imposes a structural constraint on the second-order remainder, thereby stabilizing finite-sample variance. Experiments on semi-synthetic datasets demonstrate that PEQ-Net consistently outperforms existing ICE-based methods, achieving substantial reductions in root-mean-square error, particularly when evaluating closely related policies.

AI.tldr: Proposes PEQ-Net, a policy-aware joint estimation framework that reduces variance in evaluating multiple dynamic treatment policies by sharing representations across counterfactuals, outperforming isolated estimation methods.
AI.motivation: Conventional longitudinal causal inference methods estimate each treatment policy separately, preventing information sharing across counterfactuals, which induces second-order bias and inflates finite-sample variance even after standard debiasing techniques.
AI.method: Policy-aware reparameterization of Iterative Conditional Expectation (ICE) Q-functions enabling joint estimation through shared representations, implemented as Policy-Encoded Q Network (PEQ-Net) with a shared policy encoder trained using kernel mean embeddings to reflect population-level policy dissimilarities.
AI.result: PEQ-Net consistently outperforms existing ICE-based methods on semi-synthetic datasets, achieving substantial reductions in root-mean-square error, particularly when evaluating closely related policies. The approach imposes structural constraints on second-order remainder, stabilizing finite-sample variance.
AI.conclusion: Joint estimation through policy-aware reparameterization and shared representations (PEQ-Net) provides more efficient evaluation of multiple dynamic treatment policies compared to separate estimation paradigms, with demonstrated empirical superiority and theoretical variance stabilization properties.

title: Intelligence Impact Quotient (IIQ): A Framework for Measuring Organizational AI Impact
summary: The Intelligence Impact Quotient (IIQ) is a composite metric intended to quantify the depth to which AI systems are integrated into organizational work and their impact. Rather than treating access counts or aggregate token volume as sufficient evidence of impact, IIQ combines a novelty-weighted, time-decayed token stock with usage frequency, a grace-period recency gate, organizational leverage, task complexity, and autonomy. The formulation produces a raw Intelligence Adoption Index (IAI) and a normalized 0-1000 IIQ index for comparison between heterogeneous users and units. We also derive sub-daily update rules and a bounded interpretation layer for estimated efficiency and financial impact. The paper positions IIQ as a deployment-oriented measurement framework: a formal proposal for tracking AI embedding in workflows, not a direct measure of model capability or a substitute for causal productivity evaluation. Synthetic scenarios illustrate how the revised metric distinguishes between frequent low-leverage use, semantically repetitive prompting, and more autonomous, higher-consequence AI-assisted work.

AI.tldr: The Intelligence Impact Quotient (IIQ) is a comprehensive metric system that measures how deeply AI is integrated into organizational workflows and its actual impact, going beyond simple usage statistics to account for factors like novelty, leverage, complexity, and autonomy.
AI.motivation: Traditional metrics like access counts and token volume are insufficient to measure true AI impact in organizations. There's a need for a more sophisticated framework that captures the depth of AI integration into workflows and distinguishes between superficial usage versus meaningful, high-impact applications.
AI.method: The IIQ combines multiple weighted factors: novelty-weighted time-decayed token stock, usage frequency, grace-period recency gate, organizational leverage, task complexity, and autonomy. This produces a raw Intelligence Adoption Index (IAI) which is then normalized to a 0-1000 IIQ scale. The framework includes sub-daily update rules and an interpretation layer for efficiency and financial impact estimation.
AI.result: The paper introduces the IIQ as a composite metric that can compare AI adoption across heterogeneous users and organizational units. Synthetic scenarios demonstrate how IIQ distinguishes between different usage patterns: frequent low-leverage use, semantically repetitive prompting, and more autonomous high-consequence AI-assisted work.
AI.conclusion: IIQ is positioned as a deployment-oriented measurement framework for tracking AI embedding in workflows, not as a direct measure of model capability or a substitute for causal productivity evaluation. It provides a formal proposal for better understanding AI's organizational integration and impact beyond simplistic usage metrics.

title: MetaMoE: Diversity-Aware Proxy Selection for Privacy-Preserving Mixture-of-Experts Unification
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models scale capacity by combining specialized experts, but most existing approaches assume centralized access to training data. In practice, data are distributed across clients and cannot be shared due to privacy constraints, making unified MoE training challenging. We propose MetaMoE, a privacy-preserving framework that unifies independently trained, domain-specialized experts into a single MoE using public proxy data as surrogates for inaccessible private data. Central to MetaMoE is diversity-aware proxy selection, which selects client-domain-relevant and diverse samples from public data to effectively approximate private data distributions and supervise router learning. These proxies are further used to align expert training, improving expert coordination at unification time, while a context-aware router enhances expert selection across heterogeneous inputs. Experiments on computer vision and natural language processing benchmarks demonstrate that MetaMoE consistently outperforms recent privacy-preserving MoE unification methods. Code is available at https://github.com/ws-jiang/MetaMoE.

AI.tldr: MetaMoE enables privacy-preserving unification of independently trained MoE experts using public proxy data and diversity-aware selection to approximate private data distributions.
AI.motivation: Real-world data is distributed across clients and cannot be shared due to privacy constraints, making traditional centralized MoE training infeasible. Existing MoE approaches assume centralized data access, which doesn't reflect practical privacy-preserving scenarios.
AI.method: Proposes MetaMoE with diversity-aware proxy selection from public data to approximate private distributions, uses proxies for router learning, aligns expert training with these proxies, and implements a context-aware router for expert selection across heterogeneous inputs.
AI.result: MetaMoE consistently outperforms recent privacy-preserving MoE unification methods on computer vision and natural language processing benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: MetaMoE provides an effective framework for privacy-preserving MoE unification using public proxy data, addressing practical constraints of distributed, private data while maintaining model performance.

title: Stateful Reasoning via Insight Replay
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning has become a foundation for eliciting multi-step reasoning in large language models, but recent studies show that its benefits do not scale monotonically with chain length: while longer CoT generally enables a model to tackle harder problems, on a given problem, accuracy typically increases with CoT length up to a point, after which it declines. We identify a major cause of this phenomenon: as the CoT grows, the model's attention to critical insights produced earlier in the trace gradually weakens, making those insights progressively less accessible when they are most needed. Therefore, we propose \textbf{InsightReplay}, a stateful reasoning approach in which the model periodically extracts critical insights from its reasoning trace and replays them near the active generation frontier, keeping them accessible as the reasoning scales. Extensive experiments on a $\mathbf{2}\!\times\!\mathbf{3}\!\times\!\mathbf{4}$ benchmark grid, covering model scales $\{\text{8B}, \text{30B}\}$, model families $\{\text{Qwen3.5}, \text{DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen}, \text{Gemma-4}\}$, and reasoning benchmarks $\{\text{AIME}, \text{HMMT}, \text{GPQA Diamond}, \text{LiveCodeBench v5}\}$, show that 3-round InsightReplay yields accuracy gains across \textbf{all 24 settings}, with an averaged improvement of $\mathbf{+1.65}$ points over standard CoT, and a largest single-setting gain of $\mathbf{+9.2}$ points on R1-Distill-32B's LiveCodeBench v5 subset. Our results suggest that the effectiveness of test-time scaling depends not only on how much a model reasons, but also on whether critical intermediate insights remain accessible throughout long reasoning trajectories.

AI.tldr: InsightReplay addresses the diminishing returns of longer Chain-of-Thought reasoning by periodically extracting and replaying critical insights during generation to maintain their accessibility throughout long reasoning chains.
AI.motivation: Longer Chain-of-Thought reasoning doesn't monotonically improve accuracy - it typically increases up to a point then declines, because as CoT grows, the model's attention to critical insights produced earlier in the trace gradually weakens, making those insights less accessible when needed.
AI.method: InsightReplay: a stateful reasoning approach where the model periodically extracts critical insights from its reasoning trace and replays them near the active generation frontier, keeping them accessible as reasoning scales.
AI.result: 3-round InsightReplay yields accuracy gains across all 24 experimental settings (2 model scales × 3 model families × 4 reasoning benchmarks), with average improvement of +1.65 points over standard CoT, and largest single-setting gain of +9.2 points on R1-Distill-32B's LiveCodeBench v5 subset.
AI.conclusion: The effectiveness of test-time scaling depends not only on how much a model reasons, but also on whether critical intermediate insights remain accessible throughout long reasoning trajectories. InsightReplay successfully addresses this accessibility problem.

title: Minimal-Intervention KV Retention: A Design-Space Study and a Diversity-Penalty Survivor
comment: 12 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/libophd/minimal-kv-retention
summary: KV-cache compression at small budgets is a crowded design space spanning cache representation, head-wise routing, compression cadence, decoding behavior, and within-budget scoring. We study seven mechanisms across these five families under matched mean cache on long-form mathematical reasoning (MATH-500~\cite{hendrycks2021math}) with two distilled-reasoning models (Qwen-7B and Llama-8B variants of DeepSeek-R1-Distill~\cite{deepseek2025r1}) at budgets $b \in \{64, 128\}$. All seven were rejected. We then propose $α$, a one-function modification to the TriAttention~\cite{mao2026triattention} retention scorer that replaces argmax-top-$k$ with greedy facility-location-inspired selection under a V-space redundancy penalty controlled by a single weight $λ$. A pre-registered protocol tunes $λ$ on a frozen development split and confirms on a disjoint held-out split; with $λ= 0.5$, $α$ clears Bonferroni on two of the four (model, budget) cells (Qwen $b{=}128$ and Llama $b{=}64$), no cell is significantly negative, and the pre-registered Branch~A triggers. The finding is asymmetric: a minimal scoring modification beat heavier structural redesigns in this regime, and the combined matched-memory, sympy-graded, held-out confirmation protocol is the evidence standard that made the asymmetry visible.

AI.tldr: A new KV-cache compression method called α beats seven existing approaches for long-form mathematical reasoning at small budgets, using a simple greedy facility-location-inspired selection instead of argmax-top-k.
AI.motivation: KV-cache compression at small budgets has many competing design approaches, but all seven existing mechanisms across five families were rejected in experiments. There's a need for effective compression methods that work with limited cache budgets for long-form mathematical reasoning tasks.
AI.method: Proposes α, a modification to TriAttention retention scorer that replaces argmax-top-k with greedy facility-location-inspired selection using a V-space redundancy penalty controlled by weight λ. Evaluated on MATH-500 with distilled-reasoning models (Qwen-7B and Llama-8B variants) at budgets b ∈ {64, 128}, following a pre-registered protocol that tunes λ on development split and confirms on held-out split.
AI.result: With λ=0.5, α clears Bonferroni correction in two of four (model, budget) cells (Qwen b=128 and Llama b=64), no cell is significantly negative, and the pre-registered Branch A triggers. The simple scoring modification beat heavier structural redesigns.
AI.conclusion: A minimal scoring modification outperforms more complex structural redesigns for KV-cache compression in small budget regimes. The experimental protocol with matched-memory, sympy-graded evaluation and held-out confirmation provides strong evidence standards that reveal this performance asymmetry.

title: OmniDrop: Layer-wise Token Pruning for Omni-modal LLMs via Query-Guidance
summary: Omni-modal large language models have demonstrated remarkable potential in holistic multimodal understanding; however, the token explosion caused by high-resolution audio and video inputs remains a critical bottleneck for real-time applications and long-form reasoning. Existing omni-modal token compression methods typically prune tokens at the input embedding level, relying on audio-video similarity or temporal co-occurrence as proxies for semantic relevance. In practice, such assumptions are often unreliable. To address this limitation, we propose OmniDrop, a training-free, layer-wise token pruning framework that progressively prunes audiovisual tokens within the LLM decoder layers rather than at the input-level, allowing early layers to preserve sufficient omni-modal information fusion before aggressively removing tokens in deeper layers. We further utilize text queries as guidance for modality-agnostic and task-adaptive token pruning. We also introduce a temporal diversity score that encourages balanced token survival to preserve global temporal context. Experimental results across various audiovisual benchmarks demonstrate that OmniDrop outperforms all baselines by up to 3.58 points while reducing prefill latency by up to 40% and memory usage by up to 14.7%.

AI.tldr: OmniDrop: A training-free, layer-wise token pruning framework for omni-modal LLMs that progressively prunes audiovisual tokens within decoder layers, using text queries as guidance and temporal diversity scoring to preserve global context.
AI.motivation: Omni-modal LLMs face token explosion from high-resolution audio/video inputs, creating bottlenecks for real-time applications and long-form reasoning. Existing compression methods prune at input embedding level using unreliable assumptions about audio-video similarity or temporal co-occurrence.
AI.method: 1) Progressive layer-wise token pruning within LLM decoder layers rather than input-level, 2) Text queries as guidance for modality-agnostic and task-adaptive pruning, 3) Temporal diversity score to ensure balanced token survival preserving global temporal context, 4) Training-free framework.
AI.result: Outperforms all baselines by up to 3.58 points across various audiovisual benchmarks, reduces prefill latency by up to 40%, and cuts memory usage by up to 14.7%.
AI.conclusion: OmniDrop effectively addresses token explosion in omni-modal LLMs through intelligent layer-wise pruning guided by text queries, offering significant performance improvements and efficiency gains for real-time and long-form applications.

title: Policy Optimization in Hybrid Discrete-Continuous Action Spaces via Mixed Gradients
summary: We study reinforcement learning in hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces, such as settings where the discrete component selects a regime (or index) and the continuous component optimizes within it -- a structure common in robotics, control, and operations problems. Standard model-free policy gradient methods rely on score-function (SF) estimators and suffer from severe credit-assignment issues in high-dimensional settings, leading to poor gradient quality. On the other hand, differentiable simulation largely sidesteps these issues by backpropagating through a simulator, but the presence of discrete actions or non-smooth dynamics yields biased or uninformative gradients. To address this, we propose Hybrid Policy Optimization (HPO), which backpropagates through the simulator wherever smoothness permits, using a mixed gradient estimator that combines pathwise and SF gradients while maintaining unbiasedness. We also show how problems with action discontinuities can be reformulated in hybrid form, further broadening its applicability. Empirically, HPO substantially outperforms PPO on inventory control and switched linear-quadratic regulator problems, with performance gaps increasing as the continuous action dimension grows. Finally, we characterize the structure of the mixed gradient, showing that its cross term -- which captures how continuous actions influence future discrete decisions -- becomes negligible near a discrete best response, thereby enabling approximate decentralized updates of the continuous and discrete components and reducing variance near optimality. All resources are available at github.com/MatiasAlvo/hybrid-rl.

AI.tldr: HPO is a hybrid reinforcement learning method that combines differentiable simulation for smooth components with score-function estimators for discrete actions, addressing gradient issues in hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces.
AI.motivation: Standard model-free policy gradient methods using score-function estimators suffer from severe credit-assignment issues in high-dimensional hybrid action spaces, while differentiable simulation fails when discrete actions or non-smooth dynamics create biased/uninformative gradients.
AI.method: HPO backpropagates through the simulator for smooth components, uses a mixed gradient estimator combining pathwise and score-function gradients while maintaining unbiasedness, and reformulates problems with action discontinuities into hybrid form.
AI.result: HPO substantially outperforms PPO on inventory control and switched linear-quadratic regulator problems, with performance gaps increasing as continuous action dimension grows. The mixed gradient's cross term becomes negligible near optimality, enabling approximate decentralized updates.
AI.conclusion: HPO provides an effective solution for reinforcement learning in hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces by leveraging differentiable simulation where possible while maintaining unbiased gradient estimation, with theoretical properties supporting efficient optimization near optimality.

title: From Table to Cell: Attention for Better Reasoning with TABALIGN
summary: Multi-step LLM reasoning over structured tables fails because planning and execution share no explicit cell-grounding contract. Existing methods constrain the planner to a left-to-right factorization at odds with table permutation invariance, and score intermediate states by generated content alone, overlooking cell grounding. We conduct a pilot study showing that diffusion language models (DLMs) produce more human-aligned and permutation-stable cell attention on tables than autoregressive models, with a 40.2% median reduction in attention-AUROC variability under row reordering. Motivated by this, we propose TABALIGN, a planned table reasoning framework that operationalizes the contract. TABALIGN pairs a masked DLM planner, whose bidirectional denoising emits plan steps as binary cell masks, with TABATTN, a lightweight verifier trained on 1,600 human-verified attention standards to score each step by its attention overlap with the plan-designated mask. Across eight benchmarks covering table question answering and fact verification, TABALIGN improves average accuracy by 15.76 percentage points over the strongest open-source baseline at comparable 8B-class scale, with a matched-backbone ablation attributing 2.87 percentage points of this gain to the DLM planner over an AR planner on a fixed reasoner. Cleaner DLM plans also accelerate downstream reasoning execution by 44.64%.

AI.tldr: TABALIGN: A framework using diffusion language models for better table reasoning by separating planning (as binary cell masks) from execution and verifying attention alignment with human standards.
AI.motivation: Current multi-step LLM reasoning over structured tables fails because planning and execution lack explicit cell-grounding contract, and existing methods use left-to-right factorization that conflicts with table permutation invariance while overlooking cell grounding.
AI.method: TABALIGN uses a masked DLM planner that emits plan steps as binary cell masks through bidirectional denoising, paired with TABATTN - a lightweight verifier trained on 1,600 human-verified attention standards to score each step by attention overlap with plan masks.
AI.result: Improves average accuracy by 15.76 percentage points over strongest open-source baseline across 8 benchmarks (table QA and fact verification), with 2.87pp specifically from DLM planner vs AR planner. DLM plans also accelerate downstream reasoning execution by 44.64%.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion language models produce more human-aligned and permutation-stable cell attention than autoregressive models, enabling better table reasoning through explicit cell-grounding contracts between planning and execution.

title: Language-Induced Priors for Domain Adaptation
summary: Domain adaptation faces a fundamental paradox in the cold-start regime. When target data is scarce, statistical methods fail to distinguish relevant source domains from irrelevant ones, which often leads to negative transfer. In this paper, we address this challenge by leveraging expert textual descriptions of the target domain, a resource that is often available but overlooked. We propose a probabilistic framework that translates these semantic descriptions into a choice model, namely a Language-Induced Prior (LIP), that learns the preferences from a pretrained Large Language Model (LLM). The LIP is then integrated into an Expectation-Maximization algorithm to identify source relevance. Methodologically, this framework is compatible with any parametric model where a likelihood is available. It allows the LIP to guide the selection of sources when target signals are weak, while gradually refining these choices as samples accumulate. Theoretically, we prove that the estimator roughly matches an oracle cold-start MSE under a correct prior, while remaining asymptotically consistent regardless of the quality of the LIP. Empirically, we validated the framework on a descriptive (Gaussian estimation), a predictive (C-MAPSS dataset), and a prescriptive task (MuJoCo hopper).

AI.tldr: A framework that uses expert textual descriptions to guide domain adaptation in cold-start regimes by creating Language-Induced Priors (LIPs) from LLMs to prevent negative transfer when target data is scarce.
AI.motivation: Address the fundamental paradox in cold-start domain adaptation where statistical methods fail to distinguish relevant source domains from irrelevant ones when target data is scarce, often leading to negative transfer.
AI.method: Proposes a probabilistic framework that translates expert textual descriptions into Language-Induced Priors (LIPs) using pretrained LLMs. The LIP is integrated into an Expectation-Maximization algorithm to identify source relevance, compatible with any parametric model with available likelihood.
AI.result: Theoretical proof shows the estimator roughly matches an oracle cold-start MSE under correct prior while remaining asymptotically consistent regardless of LIP quality. Empirical validation on Gaussian estimation, C-MAPSS dataset, and MuJoCo hopper tasks demonstrates effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging often-available but overlooked expert textual descriptions through LLM-derived Language-Induced Priors provides an effective solution to cold-start domain adaptation problems by guiding source selection when target signals are weak while maintaining theoretical guarantees.

title: LEMON: Learning Executable Multi-Agent Orchestration via Counterfactual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have become a strong foundation for multi-agent systems, but their effectiveness depends heavily on orchestration design. Across different tasks, role design, capacity assignment, and dependency construction jointly affect both solution quality and execution efficiency. Existing approaches automate parts of this design process, yet they often optimize these decisions partially or sequentially, and rely on execution-level feedback that provides limited credit assignment for local orchestration decisions. We propose LEMON (\textbf{L}earning \textbf{E}xecutable \textbf{M}ulti-agent \textbf{O}rchestratio\textbf{N} via Counterfactual Reinforcement Learning), an LLM-based orchestrator that generates an executable orchestration specification. The specification integrates task-specific roles, customized duties, capacity levels, and dependency structure into a single deployable system. To train the orchestrator, we augment the orchestration-level GRPO objective with a localized counterfactual signal that edits role, capacity, or dependency fields and applies the resulting reward contrast only to the edited spans. Experiments on six reasoning and coding benchmarks, including MMLU, GSM8K, AQuA, MultiArith, SVAMP, and HumanEval, show that LEMON achieves state-of-the-art performance among the evaluated multi-agent orchestration methods. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/LEMON-B23C.

AI.tldr: LEMON uses counterfactual reinforcement learning to train an LLM-based orchestrator that generates executable multi-agent orchestration specifications integrating roles, capacities, and dependencies into a single deployable system.
AI.motivation: LLMs are foundational for multi-agent systems, but effectiveness depends heavily on orchestration design. Existing approaches automate parts of design but optimize decisions partially or sequentially, with limited credit assignment for local orchestration decisions using execution-level feedback.
AI.method: LEMON (Learning Executable Multi-agent Orchestration via Counterfactual RL) generates executable orchestration specifications integrating task-specific roles, customized duties, capacity levels, and dependency structure. It augments orchestration-level GRPO objective with localized counterfactual signal that edits role, capacity, or dependency fields and applies reward contrast only to edited spans.
AI.result: Experiments on six reasoning and coding benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K, AQuA, MultiArith, SVAMP, HumanEval) show LEMON achieves state-of-the-art performance among evaluated multi-agent orchestration methods.
AI.conclusion: LEMON demonstrates that counterfactual reinforcement learning can effectively train LLM-based orchestrators to generate optimal multi-agent orchestration specifications, addressing limitations of existing approaches through localized credit assignment and integrated design optimization.

title: Matrix-Space Reinforcement Learning for Reusing Local Transition Geometry
summary: Compositional generalization in sequential decision-making requires identifying which parts of prior rollouts remain useful for new tasks. Existing methods reuse skills or predictive models, but often overlook rich local transition geometry and dynamics. We propose Matrix-Space Reinforcement Learning (MSRL), a geometric abstraction that represents trajectory segments through positive semidefinite matrix descriptors aggregating first- and second-order statistics of lifted one-step transitions. These descriptors expose shared hidden structure, support algebraic composition in an abstract matrix space, and reveal opportunities for transfer. We prove that the descriptor is well defined up to coordinate gauge, complete for the induced low-order additive signal class, additive under valid segment composition, and minimally sufficient among admissible additive descriptors. We further show that conditioning value functions on the trajectory-segment matrix yields a first-order smooth approximation of action values, enabling source-learned matrix-to-value mappings to bootstrap learning in new tasks. MSRL is plug-in compatible with standard model-free and model-based methods, while obstruction filtering rejects implausible compositions. Empirically, MSRL achieves the best average finite-budget target AUC of 0.73, outperforming MSRL from scratch (0.65), TD-MPC-PT+FT (0.63), and TD-MPC (0.57).

AI.tldr: MSRL is a geometric reinforcement learning method that uses matrix descriptors of trajectory segments to enable compositional generalization by exposing shared hidden structure and supporting algebraic composition in abstract matrix space.
AI.motivation: Current methods for compositional generalization in sequential decision-making often overlook rich local transition geometry and dynamics, focusing mainly on reusing skills or predictive models without capturing deeper structural patterns.
AI.method: MSRL represents trajectory segments through positive semidefinite matrix descriptors that aggregate first- and second-order statistics of lifted one-step transitions. These descriptors expose shared hidden structure, support algebraic composition in an abstract matrix space, and reveal transfer opportunities. The method includes obstruction filtering to reject implausible compositions.
AI.result: MSRL achieves the best average finite-budget target AUC of 0.73, outperforming MSRL from scratch (0.65), TD-MPC-PT+FT (0.63), and TD-MPC (0.57), demonstrating superior transfer learning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: MSRL provides a principled geometric abstraction for compositional generalization in RL that is theoretically grounded (proven to be well-defined, complete, additive, and minimally sufficient), practically effective, and plug-in compatible with standard model-free/model-based methods.

title: Deepchecks: Evaluating Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) augmented with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) techniques are revolutionizing applications across multiple domains, such as healthcare, finance, and customer service. Despite their potential, evaluating RAG systems remains a complex challenge due to the stochastic nature of generated outputs and the intricate interplay between retrieval and generation components. This paper introduces Deepchecks, a comprehensive framework tailored for evaluating RAG applications. Deepchecks' evaluation framework addresses RAG applications evaluation through a multi-faceted approach, root cause analysis and production monitoring. By ensuring alignment with application-specific requirements, Deepchecks framework provides a robust foundation for assessing reliability, relevance, and user satisfaction in RAG systems.

AI.tldr: Deepchecks is a comprehensive evaluation framework for RAG systems that addresses reliability, relevance, and user satisfaction through multi-faceted assessment, root cause analysis, and production monitoring.
AI.motivation: RAG systems are revolutionizing multiple domains but evaluating them is challenging due to stochastic outputs and complex retrieval-generation interplay. Current evaluation methods are insufficient for comprehensive assessment of RAG applications.
AI.method: Deepchecks employs a multi-faceted evaluation approach, root cause analysis, and production monitoring. The framework is designed to align with application-specific requirements while handling the complex nature of RAG systems.
AI.result: Deepchecks provides a robust foundation for assessing RAG systems by addressing evaluation challenges through comprehensive framework design tailored for reliability, relevance, and user satisfaction metrics.
AI.conclusion: Deepchecks represents a significant advancement in RAG system evaluation by offering a comprehensive framework that addresses the unique challenges of assessing retrieval-augmented generation applications across multiple domains.

title: Beyond Binary: Reframing GUI Critique as Continuous Semantic Alignment
comment: 28 pages including appendix. Code and BBBench benchmark to be released
summary: Test-Time Scaling (TTS), which samples multiple candidate actions and ranks them via a Critic Model, has emerged as a promising paradigm for generalist GUI agents. Its efficacy thus hinges on the critic's fine-grained ranking ability. However, existing GUI critic models uniformly adopt binary classification. Our motivational analysis of these models exposes a severe entanglement: scores for valid actions and plausible-but-invalid distractors become indistinguishable. We attribute this failure to two structural defects: Affordance Collapse--the hierarchical affordance space is compressed into 0/1 labels; and Noise Sensitivity--binary objectives overfit to noisy decision boundaries. To resolve this, we introduce BBCritic (Beyond-Binary Critic), a paradigm shift grounded in the Functional Equivalence Hypothesis. Through two-stage contrastive learning, BBCritic aligns instructions and actions in a shared Affordance Space, recovering the hierarchical structure that binary supervision flattens. We also present BBBench (Beyond-Binary Bench), the first GUI critic benchmark that pairs a dense action space with a hierarchical four-level taxonomy, enabling fine-grained ranking evaluation. Experimental results show that BBCritic-3B, trained without any extra annotation, outperforms 7B-parameter SOTA binary models. It demonstrates strong zero-shot transferability across platforms and tasks, supporting our methodological view: GUI critique is fundamentally a metric-learning problem, not a classification one.

AI.tldr: BBCritic introduces a metric-learning approach for GUI agents that replaces binary classification with hierarchical affordance space alignment, outperforming larger binary models.
AI.motivation: Existing GUI critic models use binary classification which fails to distinguish between valid actions and plausible-but-invalid distractors due to affordance collapse and noise sensitivity. Binary classification compresses hierarchical affordance space into 0/1 labels and overfits to noisy decision boundaries.
AI.method: BBCritic (Beyond-Binary Critic) introduces a paradigm shift based on the Functional Equivalence Hypothesis. It uses two-stage contrastive learning to align instructions and actions in a shared Affordance Space, recovering hierarchical structure flattened by binary supervision.
AI.result: BBCritic-3B, trained without extra annotation, outperforms 7B-parameter state-of-the-art binary models. It shows strong zero-shot transferability across platforms and tasks. The paper also introduces BBBench, the first GUI critic benchmark with dense action space and hierarchical four-level taxonomy.
AI.conclusion: GUI critique is fundamentally a metric-learning problem, not a classification problem. The proposed hierarchical affordance space alignment through contrastive learning provides superior fine-grained ranking ability compared to binary classification approaches.

title: Learning Scenario Reduction for Two-Stage Robust Optimization with Discrete Uncertainty
summary: Two-Stage Robust Optimization (2RO) with discrete uncertainty is challenging, often rendering exact solutions prohibitive. Scenario reduction alleviates this issue by selecting a small, representative subset of scenarios to enable tractable computation. However, existing methods are largely problem-agnostic, operating solely on the uncertainty set without consulting the feasible region or recourse structure. In this paper, we introduce PRISE, a problem-driven sequential lookahead heuristic that constructs reduced scenario sets by evaluating the marginal impact of each scenario. While PRISE yields high-quality scenario subsets, each selection step requires solving multiple subproblems, making it computationally expensive at scale. To address this, we propose NeurPRISE, a neural surrogate model built on a GNN-Transformer backbone that encodes the per-scenario structure via graph convolution and captures cross-scenario interactions through attention. NeurPRISE is trained via imitation learning with a gain-aware ranking objective, which distills marginal gain information from PRISE into a learned scoring function for scenario ranking and selection. Extensive results on three 2RO problems show that NeurPRISE consistently achieves competitive regret relative to comprehensive methods, maintains strong calability with varying numbers of scenarios, and delivers 7-200x speedup over PRISE. NeurPRISE also exhibits strong zero-shot generalization, effectively handling instances with larger problem scales (up to 5x), more scenarios (up to 4x), and distribution shifts.

AI.tldr: NeurPRISE: a neural surrogate method that accelerates scenario reduction for two-stage robust optimization by using GNN-Transformers to rank scenarios, achieving competitive quality with 7-200x speedup over previous heuristic.
AI.motivation: Two-stage robust optimization with discrete uncertainty is computationally prohibitive. Existing scenario reduction methods are problem-agnostic and ignore problem structure, while the effective but expensive PRISE heuristic requires solving multiple subproblems per scenario selection.
AI.method: Propose NeurPRISE: a neural surrogate model with GNN-Transformer backbone that encodes per-scenario structure via graph convolution and captures cross-scenario interactions through attention. Trained via imitation learning with gain-aware ranking objective to distill marginal gain information from PRISE.
AI.result: Extensive results on three 2RO problems show NeurPRISE achieves competitive regret relative to comprehensive methods, scales well with varying scenario numbers, delivers 7-200x speedup over PRISE, and exhibits strong zero-shot generalization to larger problem scales and distribution shifts.
AI.conclusion: NeurPRISE provides an effective neural approach to accelerate scenario reduction for two-stage robust optimization, maintaining solution quality while dramatically improving computational efficiency and showing strong generalization capabilities.

title: Guided Diffusion Sampling for Precipitation Forecast Interventions
summary: Extreme precipitation causes severe societal and economic damage, and weather control has long been discussed as a potential mitigation strategy. However, to the best of our knowledge, perturbation-based interventions for weather control using data-driven weather forecasting models have not yet been explored. While adversarial attacks also generate perturbations that alter forecasts, they aim to exploit model artifacts and do not account for physical plausibility. In this paper, we propose a gradient-based guidance framework for precipitation-reduction interventions through diffusion sampling in diffusion-based weather forecasting models. Instead of directly perturbing atmospheric states, our method steers the diffusion sampling trajectory, enabling precipitation reduction while maintaining consistency with the atmospheric distribution. To assess physical plausibility, we evaluate from three perspectives: (i) vertical and variable-wise perturbation profiles, (ii) latent-space trajectory deviation, and (iii) cross-model transferability. Experiments on extreme precipitation events from WeatherBench2 demonstrate that our method achieves effective precipitation reduction while yielding more physically plausible interventions than adversarial perturbations.

AI.tldr: Proposes a gradient-based guidance framework for precipitation reduction using diffusion-based weather forecasting models, steering diffusion sampling to reduce rainfall while maintaining physical plausibility.
AI.motivation: Extreme precipitation causes severe damage, and while weather control has been discussed as mitigation, perturbation-based interventions using data-driven forecasting models haven't been explored. Adversarial attacks exist but exploit model artifacts without considering physical plausibility.
AI.method: Gradient-based guidance framework for precipitation reduction through diffusion sampling in diffusion-based weather forecasting models. Instead of directly perturbing atmospheric states, it steers the diffusion sampling trajectory. Uses three perspectives for physical plausibility assessment: (1) vertical and variable-wise perturbation profiles, (2) latent-space trajectory deviation, and (3) cross-model transferability.
AI.result: Experiments on extreme precipitation events from WeatherBench2 show the method achieves effective precipitation reduction while yielding more physically plausible interventions than adversarial perturbations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed gradient-based guidance framework provides a novel approach to weather control interventions that maintains physical plausibility, demonstrating potential for practical precipitation mitigation strategies using data-driven weather models.

title: When Robots Do the Chores: A Benchmark and Agent for Long-Horizon Household Task Execution
summary: Long-horizon household tasks demand robust high-level planning and sustained reasoning capabilities, which are largely overlooked by existing embodied AI benchmarks that emphasize short-horizon navigation or manipulation and rely on fixed task categories. We introduce LongAct, a benchmark designed to evaluate planning-level autonomy in long-horizon household tasks specified through free-form instructions. By abstracting away embodiment-specific low-level control, LongAct isolates high-level cognitive capabilities such as instruction understanding, dependency management, memory maintenance, and adaptive planning. We further propose HoloMind, a VLM-driven agent with a DAG-based long-horizon hierarchical planner, a Multimodal Spatial Memory for persistent world modeling, an Episodic Memory for experience reuse, and a global Critic for reflective supervision. Experiments with GPT-5 and Qwen3-VL models show that HoloMind substantially improves long-horizon performance while reducing reliance on model scale. Even top models achieve only 59% goal completion and 16% full-task success, underscoring the difficulty of LongAct and the need for stronger long-horizon planning in embodied agents.

AI.tldr: LongAct is a new benchmark for evaluating planning-level autonomy in long-horizon household tasks with free-form instructions, abstracting away low-level control to focus on high-level cognitive capabilities. HoloMind is a VLM-driven agent that significantly improves long-horizon performance but current models still struggle with full-task success.
AI.motivation: Existing embodied AI benchmarks focus on short-horizon navigation/manipulation with fixed task categories, overlooking the need for robust high-level planning and sustained reasoning capabilities required for complex long-horizon household tasks.
AI.method: LongAct benchmark isolates high-level cognitive capabilities by abstracting away embodiment-specific low-level control. HoloMind agent combines: DAG-based long-horizon hierarchical planner, Multimodal Spatial Memory for persistent world modeling, Episodic Memory for experience reuse, and a global Critic for reflective supervision.
AI.result: HoloMind substantially improves long-horizon performance while reducing reliance on model scale. However, even top models achieve only 59% goal completion and 16% full-task success on LongAct, highlighting the benchmark's difficulty.
AI.conclusion: LongAct reveals significant challenges in long-horizon planning for embodied agents, and HoloMind demonstrates promising architectural improvements. The poor performance of state-of-the-art models indicates a critical need for stronger high-level planning capabilities in embodied AI systems.

title: Dynamic Latent Routing
summary: We investigate the temporal concatenation of sub-policies in Markov Decision Processes (MDP) with time-varying reward functions. We introduce General Dijkstra Search (GDS), and prove that globally optimal goal-reaching policies can be recovered through temporal composition of intermediate optimal sub-policies. Motivated by the "search, select, update" principle underlying GDS, we propose Dynamic Latent Routing (DLR), a language-model post-training method that jointly learns discrete latent codes, routing policies, and model parameters through dynamic search in a single training stage. In low-data fine-tuning settings, DLR matches or outperforms supervised fine-tuning across four datasets and six models, achieving a mean gain of +6.6 percentage points, while prior discrete-latent baselines consistently underperform SFT. Mechanistic analyses and targeted code ablations show that DLR learns structured routing behaviors with distinct causal roles.

AI.tldr: General Dijkstra Search enables optimal policy composition in time-varying MDPs; Dynamic Latent Routing uses this principle to learn discrete latent codes and routing policies for language models, outperforming supervised fine-tuning in low-data settings.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of learning optimal policies in Markov Decision Processes with time-varying reward functions, and to apply this principle to language model post-training for improved performance in low-data fine-tuning scenarios.
AI.method: Introduce General Dijkstra Search (GDS) for temporal concatenation of sub-policies in MDPs, then propose Dynamic Latent Routing (DLR) - a language-model post-training method that learns discrete latent codes, routing policies, and model parameters through dynamic search in a single training stage.
AI.result: DLR matches or outperforms supervised fine-tuning across four datasets and six models in low-data settings, achieving mean gain of +6.6 percentage points, while prior discrete-latent baselines consistently underperform SFT. Mechanistic analyses show DLR learns structured routing behaviors with distinct causal roles.
AI.conclusion: Temporal composition of optimal sub-policies via GDS enables globally optimal policies, and applying this principle through DLR provides an effective language model post-training method that excels in low-data fine-tuning by learning structured routing behaviors.

title: Cattle Trade: A Multi-Agent Benchmark for LLM Bluffing, Bidding, and Bargaining
summary: We introduce \textsc{Cattle Trade, a multi-agent benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) as agents in strategic reasoning under imperfect information, adversarial interaction, and resource constraints. The benchmark combines auctions, hidden-offer trade challenges (TCs), bargaining, bluffing, opponent modeling, and resource allocation within a single long-horizon game lasting 50--60 turns. Unlike prior agent benchmarks that test these abilities in isolation, \textsc{Cattle Trade} evaluates whether agents integrate them across a competitive, multi-agent economic game with conflicting incentives. The benchmark logs every bid, TC offer, counteroffer, and card selection, enabling behavioural analysis beyond final scores or win rates. We evaluate seven cost-efficient language models and three deterministic code agents across 242 games. Strategic coherence, in particular spending efficiency, resource discipline, and phase-adaptive bidding, is associated with rank more strongly than spending volume or any single subskill. Two heuristic code agents outperform most tested LLMs, and behavioural traces surface recurring LLM failure modes including overbidding, self-bidding, bankrupt TC initiation, and weak opponent-state adaptation. Evaluating agentic competence requires benchmarks that test the joint deployment of multiple capabilities in multi-agent environments with conflicting incentives, uncertainty, and economic dynamics.

AI.tldr: Cattle Trade is a multi-agent benchmark evaluating LLMs in complex strategic reasoning with imperfect information, adversarial interactions, and resource constraints across 50-60 turn economic games.
AI.motivation: Existing agent benchmarks test strategic abilities in isolation, but real-world competence requires integrating multiple capabilities like auctions, bargaining, bluffing, opponent modeling, and resource allocation in competitive environments with conflicting incentives.
AI.method: Created a long-horizon (50-60 turns) multi-agent economic game combining auctions, hidden-offer trade challenges, bargaining, bluffing, and resource allocation. Evaluated 7 cost-efficient LLMs and 3 deterministic code agents across 242 games with detailed behavioral logging.
AI.result: Strategic coherence (spending efficiency, resource discipline, phase-adaptive bidding) correlated more strongly with rank than spending volume or individual subskills. Heuristic code agents outperformed most LLMs. Analysis revealed recurring LLM failures: overbidding, self-bidding, bankrupt trade initiation, and weak opponent-state adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Evaluating agentic competence requires benchmarks that test joint deployment of multiple capabilities in multi-agent environments with conflicting incentives, uncertainty, and economic dynamics, rather than isolated skill testing.

title: AIM-DDI: A Model-Agnostic Multimodal Integration Module for Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction
summary: Drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction is a critical task in computational biomedicine, as adverse interactions between co-administered drugs can cause severe side effects and clinical risks. A key challenge is unseen-drug generalization, where interactions must be predicted for drugs not observed during training. Although multimodal DDI models exploit diverse drug-related information, their fusion mechanisms are often tied to specific prediction architectures, limiting their reuse across models. To address this, we propose AIM-DDI, an architecture-independent multimodal integration module that represents heterogeneous modality information as tokens in a shared latent space. By modeling dependencies across modality tokens through a unified fusion module, AIM-DDI enables model-agnostic integration of structural, chemical, and semantic drug signals across different DDI prediction architectures. Extensive evaluations across diverse DDI models and DrugBank-based settings show that AIM-DDI consistently improves prediction performance, with the strongest gains under the most challenging both-unseen setting where neither drug in a test pair is observed during training. These results suggest that treating multimodal integration as a reusable module, rather than a model-specific fusion component, is an effective strategy for robust unseen-drug DDI prediction.

AI.tldr: AIM-DDI is a reusable, architecture-independent multimodal integration module that improves drug-drug interaction prediction performance, especially for unseen drugs, by representing different drug modalities as tokens in a shared latent space.
AI.motivation: Drug-drug interaction prediction is critical but faces challenges with unseen-drug generalization. Current multimodal DDI models have fusion mechanisms tied to specific architectures, limiting reusability across different prediction models.
AI.method: Proposes AIM-DDI - an architecture-independent multimodal integration module that represents heterogeneous modality information (structural, chemical, semantic) as tokens in a shared latent space. It models dependencies across modality tokens through a unified fusion module that can work with various DDI prediction architectures.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations across diverse DDI models and DrugBank-based settings show AIM-DDI consistently improves prediction performance, with the strongest gains under the most challenging both-unseen setting (where neither drug in a test pair is observed during training).
AI.conclusion: Treating multimodal integration as a reusable module rather than a model-specific fusion component is an effective strategy for robust unseen-drug DDI prediction, suggesting that architecture-agnostic integration approaches can enhance generalization capabilities.

title: VerbalValue: A Socially Intelligent Virtual Host for Sales-Driven Live Commerce
comment: Accepted to the CVPR 2026 HiGen Workshop
summary: A skilled live-commerce host is not merely a narrator, but a sales agent who converts viewer curiosity into purchase intent through expert product knowledge, emotionally intelligent response tactics, and entertainment that serves as a vehicle for product exposure. Yet no existing AI system replicates this: conversational recommenders treat recommendation as a terminal act, while general-purpose LLMs hallucinate product claims and default to generic promotional templates that fail to engage or persuade. We present VerbalValue, a sales-conversion-oriented virtual host that turns exceptional verbal ability into real commercial value, built on three contributions. First, we construct a domain knowledge base of product specifications and a curated sales terminology lexicon that anchor product-related responses in verified expertise. Second, we collect and annotate 1,475 live-commerce interactions spanning diverse viewer intents. Third, we fine-tune a large language model on this data to deliver empathetic, commercially oriented responses, adapting to viewer intent through empathetic amplification, evidence-backed rebuttal, and humor-mediated deflection. Experiments against GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and other baselines demonstrate gains of 23% on informativeness and 18% on factual correctness, with consistent advantages in tactfulness and viewer engagement.

AI.tldr: VerbalValue is an AI system that acts as a sales-conversion-oriented virtual host for live-commerce, combining product expertise with emotionally intelligent conversational tactics to convert viewer interest into purchases.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems fail to replicate the persuasive skills of live-commerce hosts. Conversational recommenders treat recommendation as terminal acts, while general-purpose LLMs hallucinate product claims and use generic promotional templates that don't effectively engage or persuade viewers. There's a need for an AI system that combines product expertise with sales psychology and emotional intelligence.
AI.method: The system has three main components: 1) A domain knowledge base with product specifications and a curated sales terminology lexicon to ground responses in verified expertise, 2) Collection and annotation of 1,475 live-commerce interactions covering diverse viewer intents, 3) Fine-tuning a large language model on this data to deliver empathetic, commercially oriented responses using techniques like empathetic amplification, evidence-backed rebuttal, and humor-mediated deflection.
AI.result: VerbalValue outperformed GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and other baselines with significant gains: 23% improvement on informativeness and 18% improvement on factual correctness. It also showed consistent advantages in tactfulness and viewer engagement.
AI.conclusion: VerbalValue successfully bridges the gap between conversational AI and persuasive sales techniques by turning exceptional verbal ability into commercial value. The system demonstrates that specialized domain knowledge combined with emotionally intelligent conversational tactics can create virtual hosts that effectively engage viewers and drive sales conversions in live-commerce settings.

title: Nearest-Neighbor Radii under Dependent Sampling
summary: Nearest-neighbor methods are fundamental to classical and modern machine learning, yet their geometric properties are typically analyzed under independent sampling. In this paper, we study the nearest-neighbor radii under dependent sampling. We consider strong mixing dependent observations and ask whether dependence changes the scale of nearest-neighbor neighborhoods. We establish distribution-free almost sure convergence under polynomial mixing and sharp non-asymptotic moment bounds under geometric mixing. The moment bounds depend on the local intrinsic dimension rather than the ambient dimension, making the results applicable to high-dimensional data concentrated near lower-dimensional manifolds. Synthetic experiments and real-world time-series benchmarks support the theory, showing that nearest-neighbor geometry remains informative under dependence sampling.

AI.tldr: Analysis of nearest-neighbor radii under dependent sampling conditions, showing that geometric properties persist despite dependence.
AI.motivation: Traditional analysis of nearest-neighbor methods assumes independent sampling, but real-world data often exhibits dependence. The paper aims to understand whether dependence fundamentally changes the scale of nearest-neighbor neighborhoods.
AI.method: Studied nearest-neighbor radii under dependent sampling, specifically considering strong mixing dependent observations. Established distribution-free almost sure convergence under polynomial mixing and derived sharp non-asymptotic moment bounds under geometric mixing.
AI.result: The moment bounds depend on local intrinsic dimension rather than ambient dimension, making results applicable to high-dimensional data near lower-dimensional manifolds. Synthetic experiments and real-world time-series benchmarks support that nearest-neighbor geometry remains informative under dependence.
AI.conclusion: Dependence does not fundamentally alter nearest-neighbor geometry; the methods remain applicable under dependent sampling conditions, particularly when considering local intrinsic dimensionality.

title: Complacent, Not Sycophantic: Reframing Large Language Models and Designing AI Literacy for Complacent Machines
summary: Large language models are often described as sycophantic, in the sense that they appear to flatter users or mirror their beliefs. We argue that this label is conceptually misleading: sycophancy implies motives and strategic intent, which LLMs do not possess. Their behaviour is better understood as complacency, a structural tendency to agree with user input because training data, reward signals and design favour agreement and reinforcement over correction. We argue that this distinction matters. Whether developers act sycophantically or not, models themselves never are sycophants; they can only be made more or less complacent. This reframing locates agency in developers and institutions, not in the model. Because complacent models reinforce users' prior beliefs, we argue that AI literacy educational approaches should particularly focus on strategies to counter confirmation bias.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that describing LLMs as "sycophantic" is misleading, and instead proposes "complacency" as a more accurate framing, shifting agency from models to developers and institutions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to correct the conceptual misunderstanding in describing LLMs as "sycophantic" - a term that implies strategic intent and motives which LLMs don't possess. The authors aim to provide a more accurate conceptual framework for understanding LLM behavior patterns.
AI.method: The paper uses conceptual analysis and reframing, examining the structural factors in LLM development that lead to agreement with users: training data selection, reward signals, and design choices that favor agreement and reinforcement over correction.
AI.result: The key result is a conceptual reframing from "sycophancy" to "complacency" - a structural tendency for LLMs to agree with user input due to their training and design. This shift locates agency with developers and institutions rather than attributing human-like motives to models.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that AI literacy education should focus on strategies to counter confirmation bias, since complacent models reinforce users' prior beliefs. The reframing emphasizes that developers and institutions, not the models themselves, have agency in this dynamic.

title: Exemplar Partitioning for Mechanistic Interpretability
summary:
We introduce Exemplar Partitioning (EP), an unsupervised method for constructing interpretable feature dictionaries from large language model activations with $\sim 10^{3}\times$ fewer tokens than comparable sparse autoencoders (SAEs). An EP dictionary is a Voronoi partition of activation space, built by leader-clustering streamed activations within a distance threshold. Each region is anchored by an observed exemplar that serves as both its membership criterion and intervention direction; dictionary size is not prespecified, but determined by the activation geometry at that threshold. Because exemplars are observed rather than learned, dictionaries built from the same data stream are directly comparable across layers, models, and training checkpoints.
  We characterise EP as an interpretability object via targeted demonstrations of properties newly accessible through this construction, plus one head-to-head benchmark. In Gemma-2-2B, EP dictionary regions are interpretable and support causal interventions: refusal in instruction-tuned Gemma concentrates in a region whose exemplar ablation can collapse held-out refusal. Cross-checkpoint matching between base and instruction-tuned dictionaries separates the directions preserved through finetuning from those introduced by it. EP regions and Gemma Scope SAE features decompose activation space differently but agree on a shared core: $\sim 20\%$ of EP regions match an SAE feature at $F_{1} > 0.5$, and EP one-hot probes retain $\sim 97\%$ of raw-activation probe accuracy at $\ell_{0} = 1$. Nearest-exemplar distance provides a free out-of-distribution signal at inference. On AxBench latent concept detection at Gemma-2-2B-it L20, EP at $p_{1}$ reaches mean AUROC $0.881$, $+0.126$ over the canonical GemmaScope SAE leaderboard entry and within $0.030$ of SAE-A's $0.911$, at $\sim 10^{3}\times$ less build compute.

AI.tldr: EP (Exemplar Partitioning) is an unsupervised method for creating interpretable feature dictionaries from LLM activations using 1000× fewer tokens than sparse autoencoders, based on Voronoi partitions with observed exemplars rather than learned features.
AI.motivation: Current sparse autoencoders (SAEs) require massive token datasets (~10^9 tokens) for training feature dictionaries, which is computationally expensive. There's a need for more efficient methods to build interpretable feature dictionaries from LLM activations that maintain quality while being computationally cheaper.
AI.method: EP constructs a Voronoi partition of activation space by leader-clustering streamed activations within a distance threshold. Each region is anchored by an observed exemplar that serves as both membership criterion and intervention direction. Dictionary size isn't predetermined but emerges from activation geometry. Exemplars are observed rather than learned, enabling direct comparison across layers, models, and training checkpoints.
AI.result: EP dictionary regions are interpretable and enable causal interventions: in Gemma-2-2B, refusal behavior concentrates in specific regions that can be ablated. EP achieves mean AUROC 0.881 on AxBench latent concept detection, +0.126 over standard SAEs and within 0.030 of SAE-A's 0.911, while using ~1000× less build compute. EP regions and SAE features decompose activation space differently but share core features (~20% match at F1 > 0.5).
AI.conclusion: EP provides an efficient alternative to SAEs for building interpretable feature dictionaries from LLM activations, achieving comparable performance with dramatically reduced computational requirements (~1000× less compute), while maintaining interpretability and enabling novel analyses across model variants.

title: TeachAnything: A Multimodal Crowdsourcing Platform for Training Embodied AI Agents in Symmetrical Reality
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures. Accepted as an IEEE VR 2026 Poster
summary: Symmetrical Reality (SR) is emerging as a future trend for human-agent coexistence, placing higher demands on agents to acquire human-like intelligence. It calls for richer and more diverse human guidance. We introduce a three-stage demonstration paradigm integrating multimodal demonstration signals. Building on this paradigm, we developed TeachAnything, a cloud-based, crowdsourcing-oriented demonstration platform with physics simulation capable of collecting diverse demonstration data across varied scenes, tasks, and embodiments. By unifying virtual and physical interactions through both methodological design and physics simulation, the system serves as a practical foundation for developing embodied agents aligned with Symmetrical Reality.

AI.tldr: TeachAnything is a cloud-based crowdsourcing platform that collects diverse human demonstrations through a three-stage multimodal paradigm with physics simulation, serving as a foundation for developing embodied agents for Symmetrical Reality.
AI.motivation: Symmetrical Reality is emerging as a future trend for human-agent coexistence, requiring agents to acquire human-like intelligence, which calls for richer and more diverse human guidance. Current methods need improvement to collect comprehensive demonstration data across varied scenes, tasks, and embodiments.
AI.method: A three-stage demonstration paradigm integrating multimodal demonstration signals. Developed TeachAnything, a cloud-based, crowdsourcing-oriented demonstration platform with physics simulation capable of collecting diverse demonstration data across varied scenes, tasks, and embodiments. Unifies virtual and physical interactions through both methodological design and physics simulation.
AI.result: Created a practical system for collecting diverse demonstration data that serves as a foundation for developing embodied agents aligned with Symmetrical Reality. The platform enables collection of demonstration data across varied scenes, tasks, and embodiments through crowdsourcing.
AI.conclusion: TeachAnything provides a practical foundation for developing embodied agents aligned with Symmetrical Reality by unifying virtual and physical interactions through methodological design and physics simulation, addressing the need for richer and more diverse human guidance in agent development.

title: Distributionally Robust Multi-Task Reinforcement Learning via Adaptive Task Sampling
summary: Multi-task reinforcement learning (MTRL) aims to train a single agent to efficiently optimize performance across multiple tasks simultaneously.
